html:
[402]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 唱公事畢 - Official ceremony concluded; likely refers to a ritual or formal event, possibly judicial or supernatural.
  • 纷纷告退 - Scattered and departed; common expression for people leaving in a disorderly manner.
  • 仍入内殿 - Still entered the inner hall; '内殿' (neidian) refers to an inner or private hall, possibly in a temple or palace.
  • 去假面，易故衣 - Removed the mask, changed back into his old clothes; suggests a transformation from a role or disguise.
  • 童送登舆 - A child servant carried him onto the palanquin; '舆' (yu) is a palanquin or litter.
  • 十六官仍在甬道揖送 - The sixteen officials still stood in the corridor to bow and send him off; formal farewell with officials.
  • 舆前较来时多大灯一对 - In front of the palanquin, there were two large lanterns more than when he came; indicates an increase in ceremonial display.
  • 上首墨书第七殿，下首书阎罗王 - The top lantern was inscribed in black ink with 'Seventh Hall,' and the bottom one with 'Yama King'; references Buddhist/Han Chinese concept of the Underworld.
  • 直送生至家 - They directly sent him home; suggests a continuous, uninterrupted journey.
  • 释服仍卧 - He removed the ceremonial attire and lay down again; indicates returning to a normal state.
  • 有送信者在床前唤觉 - A messenger came to the bedside and called him awake; common narrative device in supernatural tales.
  • 时甫黎明 - It was just at dawn; '甫' (fu) means 'just' or 'only.'
  • 自大门至此五六层 - From the main gate to here, there are five or six layers; may refer to a multi-tiered structure or symbolic levels of the afterlife.
  • 并未闭门 - The gate was not closed; suggests that the supernatural world is accessible or not strictly guarded.
  • 何人开放 - Who opened it? Implies a mysterious or supernatural cause.
  • 姑母疾重 - My aunt is gravely ill; '疾重' indicates a severe or critical illness.
  • 奄奄一息 - On the verge of death; standard expression for someone about to die.
  • 张目执生手 - She opened her eyes and held my hand; dramatic moment in the narrative.
  • 汝来甚好，床前鬼使皆趋避矣 - You came just in time, the ghost attendants have all fled; suggests the protagonist has power or protection.
  • 勿戚戚也 - Do not be worried; colloquial expression to comfort someone.
  • 汝既不欲我死，何点我名？ - If you did not want me to die, why did you call my name? Rhetorical question, possibly implying a supernatural cause of death.
  • 天命也，我不能责汝忘亲情 - It is fate, I cannot blame you for forgetting kinship; acceptance of fate despite emotional conflict.
  • 悚然莫对 - He was startled and had no reply; common response to a shocking or emotional statement.
  • 报医来 - They announced that the doctor had arrived; '报' (bao) is a verb meaning to report or announce.
  • 至再至三 - Repeatedly, again and again; common expression for persistence.
  • 噫！止此一刻，竟不延矣 - Ah! This is the last moment, and it cannot be extended; lamentation over the inevitability of death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唱公事 - Ritual or official ceremony, possibly related to the underworld or a judicial process.
  • 第七殿 - Seventh Hall; one of the seven halls of the Underworld in Chinese Buddhist cosmology.
  • 阎罗王 - Yama King; ruler of the Underworld in Buddhist and Daoist traditions.
  • 舆 - Palanquin; used in both secular and supernatural contexts.
  • 十六官 - Sixteen officials; likely refers to officials or attendants in the Underworld.
  • 甬道 - Corridor or passage; possibly in a temple, palace, or the Underworld.
  • 送信者 - Messenger; possibly a supernatural being or human intermediary in a tale involving the afterlife.
  • 姑母 - Aunt; in classical Chinese, denotes a maternal or paternal aunt depending on context.
  • 天命 - Fate or heavenly decree; common Confucian and Daoist concept.

Proper Nouns

  • 生 - Protagonist or person; common term in classical Chinese narrative texts.
  • 第七殿 - Seventh Hall; reference to the seven halls of the Underworld in Chinese cosmology.
  • 阎罗王 - Yama King; major deity in the Chinese afterlife system.
  • 姑母 - Aunt; familial term referring to the protagonist’s aunt.
  • 送信者 - Messenger; generic term for someone who brings news or messages, possibly supernatural.

[403]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夫何以生人署阎王之职，而又不使之主政 - A rhetorical question asking why a human would be appointed to the position of Yama (阎王), the king of hell, yet not be allowed to rule.
  • 十六官皆可代也，何用生为 - A statement suggesting that if a human were appointed to Yama's position, any of the sixteen officials could replace them, questioning the purpose of living.
  • 聪明正直之为神 - A phrase indicating that being intelligent and upright is what makes one a god.
  • 此生与余一人交，未能成其为正人君子 - A statement referring to the relationship between this person and the speaker, indicating that this person has not become a virtuous and honorable person.
  • 岂阴曹之鬼犹不如生耶 - A rhetorical question asking if the ghosts in the underworld are even less virtuous than this person.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 芗厈 - The name or title of the speaker, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 阎王 - Yama, the king of hell in Chinese mythology.
  • 十六官 - The sixteen officials in the underworld, often associated with Yama.
  • 正人君子 - A term for a virtuous and honorable person.
  • 阴曹 - The underworld or the realm of the dead.
  • 阳间 - The mortal world or the world of the living.
  • 显官 - Prominent or high-ranking officials.

Proper Nouns

  • 芗厈 - The name or title of the speaker.
  • 阎王 - Yama, the king of hell.
  • 十六官 - The sixteen officials in the underworld.
  • 正人君子 - A term for a virtuous and honorable person.
  • 阴曹 - The underworld.
  • 阳间 - The mortal world.
  • 显官 - Prominent officials.

[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時少年預伏室中 - At that time, a young man had secretly hidden in the room. '預伏' implies a premeditated and hidden presence.
  • 室焚悶香 - The room was filled with a smoky incense. '焚悶香' suggests a heavy, suffocating incense that could cause drowsiness or disorientation.
  • 婦覺香甚軟 - The woman felt the incense was very soft. '軟' here likely refers to the effect of the incense, possibly making her feel drowsy or weak.
  • 伏於座 - She fell asleep on the seat. '伏' implies a state of unconsciousness or deep sleep.
  • 少年突出 - The young man suddenly appeared. '突出' suggests a quick and unexpected emergence.
  • 擁婦登床 - He took the woman to the bed. '擁' implies a forceful or aggressive action.
  • 寡婦避去 - The widow fled. '避去' suggests a quick and possibly fearful departure.
  • 婦手足麻木，口不能言 - The woman's limbs were numb, and she could not speak. '麻木' indicates a state of paralysis or loss of sensation.
  • 而心甚了了 - But her mind was very clear. '了了' means fully aware or conscious.
  • 任其淫狎 - She allowed him to act indecently. '淫狎' refers to lewd or indecent behavior.
  • 飲泣而已 - She could only weep silently. '飲泣' means to cry without making a sound.
  • 少年思得其歡心 - The young man wanted to please her. '歡心' means to gain someone's favor or approval.
  • 許與衣飾 - He promised to give her clothes and decorations. '衣飾' refers to clothing and jewelry.
  • 婦益忿怒 - The woman became even more angry. '忿怒' indicates intense anger.
  • 淚如泉涌 - Tears flowed like a spring. '泉涌' is a vivid metaphor for tears flowing uncontrollably.
  • 少年知難久留 - The young man knew he couldn't stay long. '難久留' suggests he was aware of the urgency or risk of staying.
  • 草草訖事而逸 - He hurriedly finished and escaped. '草草' implies doing something hastily or carelessly.
  • 婦醒，索寡婦不見 - When the woman woke up, she couldn't find the widow. '索' means to search for.
  • 破其室中什物而歸 - She smashed the things in the room and returned home. '什物' refers to household items or possessions.
  • 郁郁成疾 - She became ill with depression. '郁郁' indicates a state of deep sorrow or distress.
  • 女欲求計劃，以實情告 - The daughter wanted a plan and told him the truth. '計劃' means a plan or strategy.
  • 父責其不應輕出 - The father scolded her for acting recklessly. '輕出' means to act without caution.
  • 然彼以陰謀來，我當以詭計報之 - But since he came with deceit, I will repay him with cunning. '陰謀' and '詭計' both refer to deceitful or underhanded tactics.
  • 隨作一函，納女枕畔 - He then wrote a letter and placed it beside her pillow. '枕畔' means beside the pillow.
  • 能從之，則使媼來，我命兩兄助之 - If you agree, I will send the old woman, and I will have my two brothers help her. '媼' refers to an old woman or servant.
  • 父去，婦閱其書，大喜，病尋愈 - After the father left, the woman read the letter, was very happy, and her illness soon improved. '尋愈' means to recover soon.
  • 益自修饰 - She began to dress herself more carefully. '修饰' means to dress or adorn oneself.
  • 使媼强寡婦来谢过 - She sent the old woman to force the widow to come and apologize. '强' means to force or compel.
  • 与亲昵之 - She became close to her. '亲昵' means to be close or intimate.
  • 留寡妇宿 - She kept the widow overnight. '宿' means to stay overnight.
  • 密告之曰 - She secretly told her, saying. '密告' means to tell secretly.
  • 前日之事，曷不先与我谋，是所恨也 - Why didn't you consult me first about what happened yesterday? That is what I regret. '曷' means why or how.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陰謀 - Refers to deceitful or underhanded tactics, common in historical accounts of political and personal intrigues.
  • 詭計 - Refers to cunning or deceitful strategies, often used in historical narratives to describe manipulative behavior.
  • 媼 - Refers to an old woman or servant, reflecting the social hierarchy and roles of servants in ancient Chinese households.
  • 枕畔 - Refers to the side of the pillow, a common place for letters or messages in ancient Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 媼 - An old woman or servant, a common figure in ancient Chinese households.
  • 枕畔 - The side of the pillow, a specific location in the room.

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 第事巳如此 - Classical expression meaning 'But things have already come to this' or 'But matters have already reached this point.' Sets a tone of resignation or lament.
  • 縱決西江之水，難洗清白 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Even if we release the waters of the Western River, it would be hard to wash away innocence.' 'Western River' (西江) is a reference to a major river in southern China, emphasizing the impossibility of cleansing one’s reputation or innocence.
  • 良人久客不歸 - Literally 'My husband has been away for a long time and has not returned.' A common classical expression for a wife waiting for a husband who has been away.
  • 晨昏孤宿 - Refers to living alone, in solitude, during both morning and evening, indicating a life of loneliness and waiting.
  • 乃子實獲我心 - Phrase meaning 'Indeed, you have truly captured my heart.' The structure is archaic and poetic, emphasizing deep emotional connection.
  • 但面許我衣飾，何食言也 - 'But you only promised me clothing and ornaments, why have you broken your word?' The phrase '食言' (to eat one's words) is a classical idiom for breaking a promise.
  • 寡婦知其心动 - 'The widow sensed his heart was moved.' A subtle and poetic way of expressing emotional response.
  • 是人富而多情，实慕娘子 - 'This man is rich and full of emotion; he truly admires you.' '慕' is a classical term for admiration or affection.
  • 非负所许也 - 'It is not that he has broken his promise.' '负' here means to violate or betray a promise.
  • 我为导引何如 - 'What do you think of me acting as a guide?' A polite and indirect way of offering help or mediation.
  • 须防外人耳目 - 'We must be careful of outsiders' eyes and ears.' A classical way of expressing the need for discretion.
  • 来必夜深人静 - 'He must come when it is late and quiet.' This emphasizes secrecy and the need for privacy.
  • 我逐媪候门 - 'I will wait by the door for you, my lady.' '媪' is an honorific for an older woman or a term of respect for a widow.
  • 因与订期而去 - 'So they made an appointment and departed.' A common classical way of concluding a meeting or agreement.
  • 自诩有功 - 'He boasted of his own merit.' A phrase that conveys self-praise or vanity.
  • 急购鲜衣美珠 - 'He hurriedly bought fine clothes and beautiful pearls.' A vivid description of lavish preparation.
  • 使寡妇先致之 - 'He had the widow deliver them first.' A polite and indirect way of requesting something.
  • 漏四下 - 'When the fourth watch struck.' In classical Chinese, the night was divided into five watches (五更), with the fourth watch (四更) being around 1:00–3:00 AM.
  • 潜以指叩门 - 'He secretly tapped on the door with his finger.' A quiet, furtive action.
  • 即欲乱之 - 'He immediately wanted to take advantage of her.' '乱之' is a euphemistic expression for sexual advances.
  • 请同酌三杯以助兴 - 'Please drink three cups with me to raise our spirits.' A common expression in classical literature for drinking to celebrate or set the mood.
  • 案上酒食具奋 - 'The table was set with food and drink.' '奋' here is used in a classical sense to mean 'arranged' or 'prepared.'
  • 妇举杯坐怀中，口含以哺 - 'The woman raised the cup, sat in his lap, and fed him with her mouth.' A highly poetic and intimate description of the act of feeding.
  • 少年迷乱，接而吞之 - 'The young man was confused and accepted it, swallowing it.' This is a metaphorical or literal description of being overwhelmed by desire.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 寡妇 - A widow, a common figure in classical Chinese literature, often portrayed as either virtuous or tragic.
  • 良人 - A classical term for 'husband' or 'beloved.' It is a term of respect and affection.
  • 西江 - A major river in southern China, often used in classical literature as a metaphor for vastness or the impossibility of reversing a situation.
  • 鲜衣美珠 - Fine clothing and precious pearls, representing wealth and status in classical Chinese society.
  • 漏四下 - Refers to the fourth watch of the night in the traditional Chinese system of dividing the night into five watches (五更), which was used for timekeeping in ancient China.
  • 夜深人静 - A common classical expression for the quiet of the night, often used to set a romantic or clandestine scene.
  • 订期 - To make an appointment or set a meeting, a term used in both social and romantic contexts in classical literature.
  • 导引 - To guide or lead, often used in the context of helping someone achieve a goal or fulfill a desire.

Proper Nouns

  • 西江 - A river in southern China, not a specific person or place.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 徒见 - Means 'only see' or 'just see,' indicating a limited or partial observation.
  • 直入 - Means 'to enter directly' or 'to rush in.'
  • 狂奔出户 - Means 'to run wildly out of the door.'
  • 遇绳绊足而跌 - Means 'to trip over a rope and fall.'
  • 壮士缚少年手足 - Means 'the strong men tied the youth's hands and feet.'
  • 割其辫发 - Means 'cut off his queue (braid).'
  • 妇释缚纵之逸 - Means 'the woman released the bindings and let him escape.'
  • 轻叩寡妇门 - Means 'lightly knocked on the widow's door.'
  • 妇误谓少年归来 - Means 'the woman mistakenly thought the youth had returned.'
  • 俏语曰 - Means 'said in a flirtatious manner.'
  • 夜已深，正好安睡，何又草草回？想必败阵而逃也 - Means 'It's already late at night, perfect for sleeping. Why did you return so hastily? You must have fled after losing the battle.'
  • 门甫辟 - Means 'the door just opened.'
  • 壮士即刺其胸，饮刃而倒 - Means 'the strong man immediately stabbed her chest, and she fell dead.'
  • 以辫发缚妇右手数匝 - Means 'he tied her right hand with the queue several times.'
  • 归告生妇曰 - Means 'he returned and told the living woman.'
  • 大事已毕，可以复父命矣 - Means 'the great matter is finished, and we can fulfill the father's command.'
  • 盖妇两兄预伏房外而安绊绳者 - Means 'it was because the woman's two brothers had secretly set up a rope outside the room.'
  • 所哺少年之酒，哑药也 - Means 'the wine they gave the youth was drugged to make him mute.'
  • 少年逃归，哑不能言 - Means 'the youth escaped back home, mute and unable to speak.'
  • 次日亭长见寡妇被杀于门 - Means 'the next day, the village headman saw the widow killed at the door.'
  • 鸣官，验得凶刀插心 - Means 'he reported it to the authorities, who found the murder weapon stuck in her heart.'
  • 妇手握发辫，系强奸不从登时杀死者 - Means 'the woman's hand was tied with the queue, indicating that she was killed immediately for refusing rape.'
  • 密访失辫之人，获少年 - Means 'they secretly searched for the person who lost the queue and caught the youth.'
  • 无言置辩，遂按律斩之 - Means 'he had no words to defend himself and was executed according to the law.'
  • 至今楚俗，凡执奸者，必去其辫发云 - Means 'to this day, in Chu customs, anyone who catches an adulterer must cut off their queue.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 壮士 - Refers to strong men or warriors, often used in historical and literary contexts.
  • 辫发 - Refers to the queue, a traditional hairstyle for men in China until the early 20th century.
  • 寡妇 - Refers to a widow, a woman whose husband has died.
  • 亭长 - Refers to a village headman or local official in ancient China.
  • 楚俗 - Refers to the customs of Chu, an ancient Chinese state.
  • 执奸者 - Refers to someone who catches an adulterer.

Proper Nouns

  • 楚 - Refers to the ancient Chinese state of Chu.
  • 执奸者 - Refers to someone who catches an adulterer.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有潘生者 - Formal introduction of a person, meaning 'There was a man named Pan.'
  • 党讼师 - Refers to someone who is a partisan of a lawyer or a litigant, indicating a person involved in legal disputes.
  • 与庵之少尼昵甚 - Means 'he had a close relationship with the young nun in the temple.'
  • 有必正妙常之约 - Refers to a promise or agreement, possibly alluding to a specific historical or literary context (e.g., the story of 'The Tale of Yingying' where '必正' and '妙常' are characters).
  • 知法犯法 - Literally means 'knowing the law and breaking it,' indicating a deliberate violation of legal norms.
  • 佐尔主理民事乎 - Means 'how can you assist your master in handling civil cases?'
  • 关防衙署中人 - Refers to officials or staff in the government office.
  • 挟尼饮酒 - Means 'taking the nun to drink,' implying an inappropriate or improper act.
  • 何以佐尔主理民事乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how can you assist your master in handling civil cases?'
  • 传各役之首 - Means 'he summoned the leaders of the various officers.'
  • 谕令能获生奸者有赏 - Means 'he ordered that whoever could catch the adulterer would be rewarded.'
  • 不能，必革役 - Means 'if they failed, they would be dismissed.'
  • 皆领命 - Means 'they all accepted the order.'
  • 日使散役伏庵左右伺之 - Means 'they sent scattered officers to hide around the temple to watch.'
  • 生恋尼 - Means 'the man was infatuated with the nun.'
  • 役拥而入 - Means 'the officers rushed in and grabbed him.'
  • 脱生与尼衣 - Means 'they stripped the man and the nun of their clothes.'
  • 合缚之 - Means 'they bound them together.'
  • 舁出 - Means 'they carried them out.'
  • 将鸣官 - Means 'they were about to report to the authorities.'
  • 遇生党怒目拦阻 - Means 'they encountered the man's party, who glared and blocked them.'
  • 或许以重贿 - Means 'they offered a large bribe.'
  • 纷纷嬲入班馆 - Means 'they repeatedly forced them into a private room.'
  • 不使速报 - Means 'they prevented them from reporting quickly.'
  • 一生捷入潘生家 - Means 'one person quickly entered Pan's house.'
  • 告其母妻 - Means 'he told his mother and wife.'
  • 速邀亲戚入庵 - Means 'they quickly invited relatives to the temple.'
  • 嘱老尼用残香烛 - Means 'they instructed the old nun to use leftover incense and candles.'
  • 在大殿开无量寿经 - Means 'they opened the *Infinite Life Sutra* in the main hall.'
  • 旁供潘生之父神主 - Means 'they placed the spirit tablet of Pan's father beside it.'
  • 安置毕 - Means 'after everything was set up.'
  • 知会诸生 - Means 'they informed the other students.'
  • 各易素衣冠 - Means 'they all changed into plain clothes and hats.'
  • 入州廨鸣冤 - Means 'they went to the state office to lodge a complaint.'
  • 官升堂问故 - Means 'the official ascended the hall to inquire about the reason.'
  • 诸生以潘生之父百年阴寿 - Means 'the students said that Pan's father was celebrating his hundredth year of the afterlife.'
  • 同眷属戚友在尼庵礼忏 - Means 'along with family, relatives, and friends, they were at the temple to repent.'
  • 州役讹诈不遂 - Means 'the state officers tried to extort money but failed.'
  • 强以奸论 - Means 'they forced the accusation of adultery.'
  • 请官往验 - Means 'they asked the official to come and verify.'
  • 役亦以获奸报 - Means 'the officers also reported catching the adulterer.'
  • 官难辨 - Means 'the official found it difficult to distinguish.'
  • 同往验之 - Means 'he went with them to verify.'
  • 见有妇女及佛前香烛将尽 - Means 'he saw women and the incense and candles before the Buddha were almost finished.'
  • 此非仓卒所能办者 - Means 'this could not have been prepared hastily.'
  • 其为蠢役无疑 - Means 'there is no doubt that they were foolish officers.'
  • 遂杖役而释生尼 - Means 'he beat the officers and released the man and the nun.'

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 国色 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'national beauty' or 'peerless beauty,' used to describe the woman's exceptional attractiveness.
  • 为富不仁 - Phrase meaning 'rich but unkind' or 'wealthy but lacking in benevolence,' describing a person who is wealthy but morally corrupt.
  • 重贿 - Means 'to bribe heavily' or 'to pay a large sum of money as a bribe.'
  • 私之 - Means 'to have a private relationship with' or 'to engage in an illicit affair with.'
  • 往来甚密 - Means 'to have frequent and close contact' or 'to meet frequently in secret.'
  • 潜避 - Means 'to hide secretly' or 'to seek refuge in secret.'
  • 入泮 - Term referring to a scholar entering the ranks of the literati, often associated with passing an examination or receiving an official position.
  • 使媒来订婚期 - Means 'to send a matchmaker to set the wedding date.'
  • 入赘 - Means 'to marry into a family as a son-in-law,' typically referring to a man moving into his wife's family home.
  • 毙之 - Means 'to kill him' or 'to eliminate him.'
  • 两无依倚 - Means 'both without support' or 'both without a family.'
  • 于归 - Euphemism for a woman's marriage, literally meaning 'returning to her husband's home.'
  • 欣然愿赘 - Means 'to gladly agree to marry into the family.'
  • 期于清和之吉完姻 - Means 'to plan the wedding for a favorable date in the Qinghe period.'
  • 花烛 - Refers to the ceremonial candles used in traditional Chinese weddings.
  • 啧啧羡新妇美者 - Means 'to marvel at the beauty of the new bride.'
  • 畅意为欢 - Means 'to enjoy oneself fully' or 'to have a good time.'
  • 恒常羞涩 - Means 'usual shyness' or 'typical modesty.'
  • 执爵相酬饮 - Means 'to take up the cup and drink in return' or 'to toast each other.'
  • 入醉乡 - Means 'to fall into a state of drunkenness.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 粤东 - Refers to the region of Guangdong, historically known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 观剧 - Refers to attending traditional Chinese theater performances, which were popular social events.
  • 为富不仁 - Common phrase in Chinese literature and history to criticize wealthy individuals who lack moral integrity.
  • 重贿 - Reflects the historical reality of bribery and corruption, especially among the wealthy.
  • 私之 - Implies a socially unacceptable relationship, often associated with moral transgressions.
  • 潜避 - Suggests the need for secrecy, often due to social or legal consequences.
  • 入泮 - Symbolizes the achievement of a scholar, often associated with passing an examination or receiving an official position.
  • 使媒来订婚期 - Reflects the traditional role of matchmakers in arranging marriages.
  • 入赘 - Common practice in some Chinese families where the groom moves into the bride's household.
  • 毙之 - Implies a violent end, often associated with revenge or elimination.
  • 两无依倚 - Suggests a lack of family support, often used to describe individuals who are isolated or vulnerable.
  • 于归 - Euphemism for marriage, reflecting the traditional view of a woman's role in the family.

  • 候於堂 - The bride and her mother are waiting in the hall; '候於堂' (hòu yú táng) means 'waiting in the hall.'
  • 新郎所在 - The groom's whereabouts; '新郎所在' (xīn láng suǒ zài) means 'where the groom is.'

  • 鳴諸官 - Reporting to the authorities; '鳴諸官' (míng zhū guān) means 'to report to the officials.'
  • 官訊客 - The officials questioned the guests; '官訊客' (guān xùn kè) means 'the officials questioned the guests.'
  • 還尸而已 - The bride and her mother requested to recover the body; '還尸' (huán shī) means 'to recover the body,' and '而已' (ér yǐ) means 'only' or 'just.'
  • 官至河涘驗勘 - The officials went to the riverbank to investigate; '官至河涘' (guān zhì hé sì) means 'the officials went to the riverbank,' and '驗勘' (yàn kān) means 'to investigate' or 'to examine.'
  • 流長源遠 - The river is long and deep; '流長源遠' (liú zhǎng yuán yuǎn) means 'long and deep.'
  • 無從求尸 - The body could not be found; '無從求尸' (wú cóng qiú shī) means 'could not find the body.'
  • 疑獄 - A case that remains unresolved; '疑獄' (yí yù) means 'doubtful case' or 'unresolved case.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 內宅 - The inner quarters of a household, typically reserved for women and family members; in traditional Chinese society, the inner quarters were a private and sacred space.
  • 新郎 - The groom in a wedding ceremony; in traditional Chinese weddings, the groom would wear specific attire, and his actions in this story are highly unusual and disturbing.
  • 狂躍而出 - The groom leaping out in a frenzied manner; in Chinese culture, such behavior would be seen as highly abnormal and possibly indicative of mental distress or supernatural influence.
  • 大河 - A large river, which in Chinese culture often symbolizes both life and death; the mention of the Yellow River (黃河, Huáng Hé) is particularly significant, as it is one of the most important rivers in Chinese history and mythology.
  • 屍所在 - The location of the body; in Chinese culture, the recovery of a body is a critical concern, especially in cases of sudden death, as it affects the family's ability to perform proper funeral rites.
  • 至親好友見死不救 - Close relatives and friends did not come to the rescue; in Chinese culture, the duty to help family and friends in times of crisis is deeply ingrained, and the accusation of neglect would be taken very seriously.
  • 鳴諸官 - Reporting to the authorities; in traditional Chinese society, reporting to the authorities was a formal and serious action, often involving legal proceedings.
  • 官訊客 - The officials questioned the guests; in traditional Chinese legal practices, questioning witnesses was a standard part of the investigation process.
  • 還尸而已 - The bride and her mother requested to recover the body; in Chinese culture, the recovery of a body is essential for performing proper funeral rites, and the limited scope of their request suggests a focus on practical matters rather than broader justice.
  • 疑獄 - A case that remains unresolved; in traditional Chinese legal practices, unresolved cases were often left to the judgment of the community or higher authorities, and they could have long-lasting social and legal implications.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天道昭彰 - A belief in divine retribution or justice, a common theme in Chinese philosophy and literature.
  • 大盗亦戒采花 - Suggests that even notorious criminals are cautious about certain crimes, reflecting societal norms and fears.
  • 新郎投河而死 - Describes a specific event, likely a local incident, that became a mystery.
  • 众目观瞻 - Indicates the public nature of the event, suggesting that it was a matter of public interest.
  • 与妇女何尤 - Implies a cultural context where women are often blamed or suspected in such cases.
  • 遂成疑案 - Reflects the complexity and difficulty of solving certain crimes in ancient times.
  • 揣新郎之发狂 - Suggests a possible mental health issue, which was a concern in ancient Chinese society.
  • 或以药酒为之 - Implies the use of poison, a common method of murder in ancient times.

  • 不能破其奸 - Indicates the limitations of the legal system in uncovering the truth.

  • 可谓有胆有识 - Praises the magistrate's actions, reflecting the importance of courage and wisdom in leadership.
  • 床下之男履不露 - Suggests a hidden clue, which is a common element in mystery stories.
  • 何从发其覆乎 - Reflects the difficulty in solving complex cases, a recurring theme in Chinese literature.
  • 我故曰：“天道也。” - Concludes that divine justice prevailed, a common belief in Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 阴谋诡计，惟图色为甚 - This phrase introduces the main theme of the text, emphasizing the intensity of the motive.
  • 天道昭彰，竟无不破之案 - This clause suggests that divine justice is inevitable, even in the most hidden cases.
  • 大盗亦戒采花 - This clause implies that even notorious criminals are cautious about certain crimes.
  • 庸庸者流，竟谓新郎投河而死，众目观瞻，与妇女何尤？遂成疑案 - This long sentence describes the initial reaction to the event and the resulting mystery.
  • 揣新郎之发狂也，或以药酒为之，疑女有故 - This clause suggests possible explanations for the groom's actions.
  • 不能破其奸，敢讯诸乎 - This clause indicates the limitations of the investigation.
  • 令之勘访搜寻，可谓有胆有识 - This clause praises the magistrate's actions.
  • 然使床下之男履不露，何从发其覆乎 - This clause suggests a hidden clue and the difficulty in uncovering the truth.
  • 我故曰：“天道也。” - This final clause concludes that divine justice prevailed.

  • 司阍者盛服据上坐 - The gatekeeper was dressed in fine attire and seated in the upper seat, indicating a position of authority.
  • 问其仆曰 - He asked his servant, a common phrase in Classical Chinese.
  • 某官已至乎 - Has the official arrived? a question about the arrival of a person.
  • 仆乃引商入 - The servant then led Shang in, indicating a guide or escort.
  • 商见陈设眩目 - Shang saw the display and was dazzled, where '眩目' means to be overwhelmed by the sight.
  • 人物轩昂 - The people were dignified and impressive, indicating a sense of nobility and respect.
  • 意为即方伯 - Thinking it was the provincial governor, a mistaken assumption.
  • 趋入拜脆 - Rushed in to pay his respects, where '拜脆' is a more formal way of bowing.
  • 献履历 - Presented his resume, indicating the submission of personal credentials.
  • 司阍者笑曰 - The gatekeeper laughed and said, a common phrase for a response.
  • 误矣 - It was a mistake, indicating an error.
  • 敝主人不在此 - My master is not here, where '敝' is a humble term for one's own.
  • 且请少憩无躁 - Please rest for a moment and do not be anxious, a polite request for patience.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 众尤商曰 - The crowd's reproach suggests a social context where Shang's behavior was under scrutiny.
  • 国恤闻 - The period of national mourning indicates a significant event in the community, such as the death of a ruler or a prominent figure.
  • 官皆白衣冠 - The practice of wearing white clothes and hats during mourning is a traditional Chinese custom.
  • 避舍二十七日后 - The specific duration of twenty-seven days for avoiding residences is a traditional period of mourning.
  • 素服顶帽见客 - Wearing plain clothes and hats to meet guests is a sign of respect and humility during mourning.
  • 方伯 - A term for a provincial governor, indicating a high-ranking official.
  • 宪台 - A term for higher authorities, often referring to the central government or high-ranking officials.
  • 藩署 - The provincial office, a significant administrative center.
  • 司阍者 - The gatekeeper, a position of authority within the office.
  • 观察 - A term for a governor, indicating a high-ranking official.

Proper Nouns

  • 众 - The crowd, a collective noun.
  • 商 - Shang, the main character.
  • 方伯 - The provincial governor.
  • 宪台 - The higher authorities.
  • 藩署 - The provincial office.
  • 司阍者 - The gatekeeper.
  • 观察 - The governor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 司阍 - Refers to a gatekeeper or doorkeeper, a common official position in ancient China.
  • 方伯 - A term for a high-ranking official or governor in ancient China.

  • 客室 - A guest room or reception hall.

  • 拂然大怒 - To become extremely angry.

  • 遂休之 - To dismiss or release the person, indicating that the matter was resolved.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 司阍 - The role of a gatekeeper in ancient Chinese society, often responsible for maintaining order and security at official residences.
  • 方伯 - A high-ranking official, often a governor or regional administrator, who held significant power and authority.
  • 随官 - Attendants or subordinates who served the higher officials, often performing various tasks and duties.
  • 客堂 - A formal reception area or guest room, often decorated with specific items to reflect the status of the host.
  • 铺垫皆黑麻为之 - The use of black hemp padding, which was a common material in ancient Chinese households, often associated with simplicity and modesty.
  • 金其顶者 - The golden topknot was a symbol of high rank and status in ancient China, often worn by officials.
  • 大人该死 - A respectful way of apologizing to a superior, acknowledging one's mistake and expressing deep respect.
  • 散卑府之朝珠者 - The loss of the official's ceremonial beads was a serious offense, indicating a lack of respect or carelessness.
  • 仍使归市可也 - The decision to send the person back to the market reflects a pragmatic approach to dealing with the situation, emphasizing the importance of maintaining order and respect.

Proper Nouns

  • 朝珠 - The ceremonial beads worn by officials in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是躁人也 - Judgmental phrase meaning 'this is a restless person' or 'this is a hasty person'.
  • 不第杂职中有之 - Means 'not only are there such people among the miscellaneous officials,' emphasizing the widespread nature of the trait.
  • 问及峨眉山猴子究有多大 - Literal question about the size of monkeys on Mount Emei, used humorously to highlight the official's ignorance.
  • 同大人大 - The official's response, which is a play on words. '同大人' can be interpreted as 'the same as a great person' or 'as big as a great person'.
  • 制军哂之 - Means 'the governor laughed at him,' indicating the governor's amusement at the official's response.
  • 尹自觉谬，惶然改口曰：“与卑职一般大。” - The official realizes his mistake and changes his answer to 'the same size as me,' showing his confusion and embarrassment.
  • 皆传为笑柄 - Means 'all became a laughingstock,' indicating the widespread ridicule of the official.
  • 余故曰躁则妄，妄则言多谬 - A concluding statement meaning 'I therefore say that restlessness leads to rashness, and rashness leads to many mistakes.'
  • 当以三愆为戒，为之徐徐云尔 - Means 'one should take three faults as a warning and act slowly and carefully.'
  • 昔有人戒友之躁者 - Refers to a past incident where someone tried to warn a friend about their restlessness.
  • 其人衣适着火 - Means 'the person's clothes caught fire,' setting up the humorous situation.
  • 友见之而不言 - Means 'the friend saw it but did not speak,' indicating the friend's adherence to the advice to act slowly.
  • 火焚及身，怒其仆不早为扑灭 - Means 'the fire burned him, and he was angry that his servant did not put it out earlier,' showing the consequences of the friend's inaction.
  • 若遇是人，又将何说 - Means 'if one encounters such a person, what can one say?' indicating the difficulty in dealing with such a person.
  • 然则举世皆谬，若欲不谬，何如出世 - A concluding statement meaning 'then the whole world is wrong, if one wants not to be wrong, how about leaving the world?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 制军 - Refers to a high-ranking military official, specifically the governor-general.
  • 藩臬道 - Refers to high-ranking officials in the provincial government, including the governor, the censor, and the provincial commissioner.
  • 三愆 - Refers to three faults or mistakes, often used in classical Chinese to denote specific types of errors.
  • 出世 - A Buddhist term meaning 'to leave the world' or 'to renounce the world,' indicating a retreat from worldly affairs.

Proper Nouns

  • 制军 - Governor-general.
  • 藩臬道 - High-ranking officials in the provincial government.
  • 三愆 - Three faults or mistakes.
  • 出世 - Leaving the world or renouncing the world.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 贼以其处子碍羞，允之 - The bandit leader, considering the girl's virginity a matter of shame, allowed it.
  • 女乃盥沐凝妆 - The girl then washed herself and carefully put on makeup.
  • 以己之装饰饰贼尸 - She dressed the bandit's body with her own clothes.
  • 傅粉涂朱 - She applied powder and rouge.
  • 剪己鬓贴其首 - She cut her own hair and attached it to his head.
  • 加以钗钿 - She added hairpins and ornaments.
  • 望之宛似女身 - It looked like her own body.
  • 衣贼帅衣冠佩剑 - She dressed in the bandit leader's clothes and wore his sword.
  • 薄暮出，呼马执旗而遁 - At dusk, she left, calling for a horse and holding a flag as she fled.
  • 其亲信者候之，彻夜不归 - His trusted followers waited for him, and he did not return all night.
  • 大营有令，众议将军昨何往，谅夫人知之 - The main camp issued a command, and everyone wondered where the general had gone yesterday, thinking that his wife must know.
  • 嗣是贼营得妇女，不敢留，掠人之风，因之稍戢 - Afterward, when the bandit camp captured women, they dared not keep them, and the practice of seizing people gradually subsided.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贼 - Refers to a bandit or rebel leader, likely from a historical or fictional context.
  • 处子 - Refers to a virgin, emphasizing the girl's purity.
  • 傅粉涂朱 - A reference to traditional Chinese beauty practices, using powder and rouge to enhance appearance.
  • 钗钿 - Refers to traditional Chinese hair ornaments, symbolizing femininity and status.
  • 衣冠佩剑 - A reference to formal attire and weaponry, often associated with authority and leadership.
  • 薄暮 - A time-specific term meaning 'dusk,' often used in classical literature to evoke a sense of transition or urgency.
  • 亲信者 - Refers to trusted followers or subordinates, common in military or leadership contexts.
  • 大营 - Refers to the main camp or headquarters of a military force.
  • 嗣是 - Means 'afterward' or 'thereafter,' indicating a change in behavior or practice.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence structure is compact, with the bandit leader's decision to allow the girl's virginity being the focus.
  • The sequence of actions (washing, applying makeup, seeing a blade, and hiding it) is linked but could be read as a series of events.
  • This long sentence describes the girl's meticulous efforts to disguise the bandit's body as her own.
  • The sentence describes the girl's escape, with multiple actions linked in a single clause.
  • The sentence is elliptical, with the bandit leader's absence being the focus.
  • The sentence is a command followed by a question, with the assumption that the wife knows the general's whereabouts.
  • The sentence describes a change in behavior, with the bandit camp's practice of seizing women becoming less frequent.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以甘言悦贼帅 - Literal phrase meaning 'to use sweet words to please the bandit leader,' where '甘言' (sweet words) is a euphemism for flattery or deception.
  • 使不备反而刺之 - Means 'to make them unprepared and then stab them,' where '不备' (unprepared) refers to a lack of vigilance, and '刺之' (stab them) implies a sudden attack.
  • 颠倒阴阳 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to reverse the natural order of yin and yang,' symbolizing a reversal of the expected or conventional.
  • 悠然而逝 - Means 'to drift away effortlessly,' suggesting a sudden and unexpected disappearance.
  • 出人意外 - Means 'to go beyond people's expectations,' emphasizing the surprising nature of the event.
  • 年逾八旬乃卒 - Means 'to live past eighty years before dying,' where '逾' (past) indicates exceeding a certain age, and '乃' (then) marks the completion of the action.
  • 以带联结众身 - Means 'to link together many bodies with a rope,' where '联结' (link together) implies unity or connection.
  • 各持刀作方阵而行 - Means 'each holding a knife and forming a square formation while moving,' where '方阵' (square formation) refers to a military or defensive arrangement.
  • 围之不克 - Means 'to surround them but fail to conquer,' where '不克' (fail to conquer) indicates an unsuccessful attempt.
  • 以火枪轰击而并殪之 - Means 'to attack with fire guns and kill them all at once,' where '并殪' (kill them all at once) emphasizes the simultaneous nature of the deaths.
  • 惜乎，不得智女为之帅 - Means 'alas, there was no wise woman to lead them,' where '惜乎' (alas) expresses regret, and '不得' (no) indicates the absence of something.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贼帅 - Refers to the leader of a bandit group, a common figure in historical accounts of conflict and rebellion.
  • 节烈妇女 - Refers to women who are both chaste (节) and heroic (烈), a term often used in historical and literary contexts to praise women's moral and physical courage.
  • 颠倒阴阳 - A concept from Chinese philosophy and cosmology, where 'yin' and 'yang' represent complementary forces in nature. Reversing them symbolizes a disruption of the natural order.
  • 火枪 - Refers to early firearms, which were introduced to China during the late Ming and Qing dynasties.
  • 智女 - Refers to a wise and capable woman, a term that emphasizes intelligence and leadership.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 以甘言悦贼帅，使不备反而刺之 - This phrase describes a strategy of deception and surprise, where the bandit leader is lulled into a false sense of security before being attacked.
  • 颠倒阴阳，悠然而逝 - This phrase is metaphorical and somewhat ambiguous, as it describes an event that defies natural expectations.
  • 使不及追，且能全他人之节 - This phrase is elliptical but means that the action was so swift that it could not be pursued, and it preserved the chastity of others.
  • 以带联结众身，各持刀作方阵而行 - This phrase describes a coordinated military formation, where women are united and armed.
  • 惜乎，不得智女为之帅 - This phrase expresses regret about the absence of a wise leader, which would have made the situation even more remarkable.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷三 - Indicates the third volume or section of a larger work.
  • 陆清献公遗事 - Refers to the posthumous biography or anecdotes of Lu Qingxian (陸清獻).
  • 配享圣庙 - Means 'shared in the temple of the Sage,' indicating that Lu Qingxian was honored in the Confucian temple.
  • 以理学名家 - Means 'famous for his mastery of Neo-Confucianism.'
  • 无幕友官亲 - Means 'without any staff or relatives in official positions,' indicating that Lu Qingxian did not rely on a retinue or family connections.
  • 二仆与官，三人而已 - Means 'only two servants and myself, three in total,' emphasizing the simplicity of his administration.
  • 凡莅民，以省事为第一关 - Means 'in governing the people, the first priority is to minimize affairs,' highlighting the importance of efficiency in governance.
  • 我无多事，不须若干人祗候 - Means 'I have few matters, so I do not need many people to wait on me,' reinforcing the idea of minimal bureaucracy.
  • 或农或贾，各就所能，以养尔父母妻子 - Means 'whether farming or trading, each should do what they can to support their parents and children,' promoting self-reliance and family responsibility.
  • 已领工食，应为国家办事，不能尽去 - Means 'those who have received their salaries should serve the state, and cannot all be dismissed,' indicating a pragmatic approach to employment.
  • 每日酌留书吏二人，衙役四名，轮班听事，余各执业，勿远离也 - Means 'each day, two clerks and four clerical assistants are kept on duty in shifts, while the rest carry out their duties and should not stray far,' detailing the daily routine of the administration.
  • 暖阁之内，左右二几，一具律，一具平码 - Means 'inside the warm pavilion, there are two desks, one with legal codes and one with weights and measures,' describing the office setup.
  • 完粮银者，当堂弹兑，余厘毫，必还之 - Means 'those who pay taxes should be weighed and exchanged in person, and any excess should be returned,' ensuring fair and transparent tax collection.
  • 投呈者，呼至案前，细为评点 - Means 'those who submit petitions are called to the desk for detailed review,' indicating a thorough and personal approach to handling official matters.
  • 某一节，人错待汝；某一节，无此情理，谎词也 - Means 'in one part, you were wronged by someone; in another, there is no such reason, and it is a lie,' providing a nuanced evaluation of the petition.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 應訊者 - A person summoned for interrogation or trial; sets the context of a legal proceeding.
  • 書票钤印过朱 - Legal term referring to the process of writing a summons, stamping it, and passing it through official channels; '过朱' may imply a formal approval or verification step.
  • 差原告往传被告到案 - 'Differ the plaintiff to go and summon the defendant to appear in court'; legal procedural phrase.
  • 曲为开导 - 'To gently persuade' or 'to carefully guide'; indicates a conciliatory approach.
  • 尔原被非亲即故，非故即邻 - Classical rhetorical structure meaning 'you [the defendant] and the plaintiff are either relatives, acquaintances, or neighbors'; uses repetition and contrast to emphasize the closeness of the relationship.
  • 平日皆情之至密者 - 'In the past, you were the closest of friends or kin'; '情之至密' is a metaphor for deep emotional ties.
  • 一讼之兴，未见曲直 - 'Once a lawsuit begins, the truth of who is right or wrong is not yet known'; '曲直' is a classical term for 'right and wrong'.
  • 吏有纸张之费，役有饮食之需 - 'The officials have the cost of paper, and the clerks have the need for food'; list of expenses incurred by the court.
  • 证佐之亲友，必需酬劳 - 'The friends and relatives who serve as witnesses must be compensated'; reflects the social structure of witness testimony in classical legal systems.
  • 往往所费多于所争 - 'Often, the cost of the lawsuit exceeds the value of the dispute'; common saying used to warn against litigation.
  • 守候公门，废时失业 - 'Waiting at the public gate, wasting time and losing one’s livelihood'; metaphor for the personal cost of legal proceedings.
  • 一经官断，须有输赢 - 'Once the court makes a ruling, there must be a winner and a loser'; reflects the adversarial nature of classical legal systems.
  • 从此乡党变为讼仇 - 'From this point on, neighbors become litigants and enemies'; '乡党' refers to people from the same local community.
  • 薄产亿为乌有 - 'A small estate becomes as if it never existed'; metaphor for financial ruin.
  • 切齿数世，悔之晚矣 - 'Bitter hatred for generations, regret comes too late'; classical expression of long-term consequences.
  • 即如此案某人故薄待某人 - 'In this case, such-and-such a person was treated lightly by another'; '故薄待' implies intentional or habitual neglect.
  • 检律例以指示之 - 'To check the legal code and show them the consequences'; refers to consulting legal statutes.
  • 罪应笞杖 - 'The crime deserves a beating with the rod or stick'; classical form of punishment.
  • 但国法不加有礼之人 - 'But national law does not punish those who are respectful and courteous'; legal principle emphasizing morality.
  • 某合与某叩首服理 - 'So-and-so should kneel and submit to the reason'; formal act of reconciliation.
  • 回去静思三日 - 'Go home and reflect for three days'; traditional method of encouraging self-reflection.
  • 倘彼此豁然，来授结可也 - 'If both parties come to an understanding, then they may submit a reconciliation statement'; procedural step in the legal process.
  • 感激涕零 - 'Touched to tears'; strong emotional response.
  • 情愿当堂具结，和好如初 - 'Willing to submit a reconciliation statement in court and reconcile as before'; resolution of the case.
  • 互相劝勉勿讼 - 'They then encourage each other not to sue'; moral conclusion.
  • 门庭如水 - 'The gate is as empty as water,' meaning no more disputes.
  • 堂可张罗 - 'The hall can now be set up for other matters'; metaphor for the court resuming normal function.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 应讯者 - Refers to someone summoned for interrogation, indicating a formal legal process.
  • 书票钤印过朱 - Formal legal procedure in the classical Chinese legal system, involving the writing, stamping, and verification of a summons.
  • 乡党 - Term used in classical Chinese to refer to people from the same local community, emphasizing social and familial ties.
  • 叩首服理 - Traditional act of submission and reconciliation, where one kneels and bows to acknowledge another's point of view.
  • 具结 - Legal term for a written statement of reconciliation or agreement.
  • 笞杖 - Classical forms of corporal punishment used in Chinese law.
  • 律例 - Legal statutes or codes, such as the *Da Qing Lüli* (Great Qing Legal Code), used in legal proceedings.
  • 国法 - National law, as opposed to moral or social norms.
  • 有礼之人 - Term emphasizing moral conduct and respect, which was highly valued in classical Chinese society.
  • 静思三日 - Traditional practice of reflection and meditation, used to encourage reconciliation.
  • 门庭如水 - Metaphor for peace and quiet, implying the absence of disputes or litigation.
  • 堂可张罗 - Metaphor for the resumption of normal court operations, once disputes have been resolved.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公无事，带一书一役 - Means 'when the official had no urgent matters, he would take along a book and a servant,' indicating a routine practice.
  • 挨村讲乡约 - Means 'to visit villages and explain the village regulations,' emphasizing the official's role in community governance.
  • 训以孝弟忠信，节义廉耻 - A classical list of virtues: filial piety, fraternal duty, loyalty, trustworthiness, righteousness, propriety, integrity, and shame. These virtues are central to Confucian ethics.
  • 必引经据典，参以时事 - Means 'he would always cite classical texts and relate them to current events,' showing the integration of tradition and contemporary issues.
  • 民皆乐听，浅直易从，无窒碍难行者 - Means 'the people were all happy to listen, finding the teachings simple, direct, and easy to follow, with no obstacles or difficulties.'
  • 遇孝慈节义之人，立即表扬，且为之咨嗟叹赏曰：“我所不及。” - Means 'when he encountered people who were filial, kind, righteous, and virtuous, he would immediately praise them, and he would sigh and admire, saying, 'I am not as good as they are.' This highlights the official's respect for moral exemplars.
  • 凡获小偷到案 - Means 'whenever a thief was caught,' indicating a common occurrence.
  • 汝亦有人心者也，何至为此？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'You are also a human being, how could you do such a thing?' It appeals to the thief's sense of humanity.
  • 为贫所迫耳 - Means 'It's just that I was forced by poverty,' explaining the thief's motivation.
  • 为利之最厚者，莫如纺织，且人人能为之 - Means 'The most profitable thing is weaving, and everyone can do it,' suggesting a practical solution to poverty.
  • 随命仆市棉花斤余 - Means 'He then ordered his servant to buy several pounds of cotton,' indicating the official's practical approach.
  • 使偷在堂右，教以纺花之法 - Means 'He made the thief stand to the right of the hall and taught him the method of spinning cotton,' showing the official's hands-on teaching.
  • 能，即释汝；不能，即惰也，罪加倍 - Means 'If you can do it, I will release you; if you cannot, it is due to laziness, and the punishment will be doubled,' emphasizing the importance of effort.
  • 愿速释，无不留心学习 - Means 'The thief wanted to be released quickly and paid close attention to learning,' showing the thief's motivation to improve.
  • 数日间，即能为矣 - Means 'Within a few days, he could do it,' indicating rapid progress.
  • 此赀本不过数百钱耳，今数日内，循环例换，已赢余若干，除汝饭食外，尚剩有数百钱 - Means 'The capital required is only a few hundred coins. Within a few days, through repeated exchanges, he has already made a profit. After deducting your food expenses, there are still several hundred coins left,' explaining the financial benefit.
  • 汝回去执业。倘再犯，不恕汝也 - Means 'Go back and practice your trade. If you commit another crime, I will not forgive you,' emphasizing the importance of reform.
  • 间有犯，则杖而后教之，在堂上纺花一月 - Means 'If he occasionally committed another crime, he would be beaten and then taught, spinning cotton in the hall for a month,' showing the official's method of punishment and rehabilitation.
  • 是不能改矣 - Means 'He cannot be reformed,' indicating a final judgment.
  • 使二役挟之急行千步，以热醋灌之，一碗饮至半，使一人突拍其背，则嗽终身不愈，不能作贼矣，仍纺花以没世 - Means 'He would order two servants to forcibly make him run a thousand steps, then pour hot vinegar down his throat until he had drunk half a bowl. One person would suddenly slap his back, causing him to cough for the rest of his life, unable to be a thief anymore. He would continue spinning cotton until the end of his life,' describing a severe but effective method of deterrence and rehabilitation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乡约 - Refers to village regulations or community agreements, which were important in traditional Chinese society for maintaining order and promoting moral behavior.
  • 孝弟忠信，节义廉耻 - These are the core virtues of Confucian ethics, emphasizing personal and social responsibilities.
  • 引经据典 - Refers to the practice of citing classical texts and historical examples, which was a hallmark of scholarly discourse in China.
  • 纺织 - Weaving was a common and profitable craft in traditional Chinese society, often practiced by women.
  • 杖而后教之 - This refers to the practice of corporal punishment followed by education, which was a common method of discipline in traditional Chinese law.
  • 热醋灌之 - Pouring hot vinegar down the throat was a form of corporal punishment used to deter criminal behavior.
  • 纺花以没世 - Continuing to spin cotton until the end of one's life suggests a lifelong commitment to a productive and virtuous life.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence describing the official's routine and the effectiveness of his teachings can be broken down into several parts for clarity.
  • The sequence of dialogue showing the official's approach to dealing with thieves, emphasizing practical solutions and moral reasoning.
  • The description of the official's hands-on teaching method and the thief's rapid progress.
  • The explanation of the financial benefit of weaving and the consequences of repeated crimes.
  • The description of the official's method of punishment and rehabilitation, emphasizing the severity and effectiveness of the measures.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 时尚未设养廉 - Indicates the absence of a salary or allowance system for officials.
  • 奉文有摊捐款 - Refers to the practice of officials receiving funds from localities to support their duties.
  • 具文申请曰 - Means to formally write an application or petition.
  • 职奉银四十五两，仅敷自食，所需捐款，动钱粮耶？抑杂税耶？ - Expresses that the official receives only enough money to live on and inquires about the source of additional funds, whether from regular taxes or miscellaneous levies.
  • 道府寿，以挂面十束、筋烛一对，躬亲致祝 - Describes a ritual where local officials present gifts such as bundles of noodles and pairs of candles to celebrate a superior's birthday.
  • 上游皆恶其执，甄别已之 - Indicates that superiors disapprove of the official's strict adherence to rules and thus exclude him from promotion.
  • 后任甫至，即有报案者，系以斧杀人于旷野，不知凶手为谁 - Describes a situation where a new official arrives and immediately receives a report of a murder in a field, with no knowledge of the perpetrator.
  • 囹圄空虚，案无留牍 - Means that the prison is empty and there are no pending cases, suggesting effective governance.

  • 命案有无，偶然耳 - Suggests that the occurrence of a murder case is random or coincidental.

  • 案无事由，杀无证佐 - Means that the case lacks details and there is no evidence or witnesses to support the claim.
  • 前官，圣贤也，岂肯冤人 - A statement expressing that the previous official, being a sage, would not wrongfully accuse someone.
  • 带汝见前官，谅必有说 - Suggests that the new official believes the previous one will provide a solution.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 养廉 - Refers to the practice of providing salaries or allowances to officials to prevent corruption.
  • 摊捐款 - A practice where localities contribute funds to support officials.
  • 道府寿 - A ritual where local officials celebrate the birthday of a superior.
  • 挂面十束、筋烛一对 - Gifts traditionally presented during birthday celebrations.
  • 甄别已之 - A term indicating that an official has been excluded from promotion due to poor performance or behavior.
  • 命案 - Refers to a murder case.
  • 春风化雨 - A metaphor for benevolent governance or education that nurtures and transforms people.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人之所以異於禽獸者，廉恥二字 - Classical statement emphasizing that what distinguishes humans from animals is the sense of shame and honor.
  • 若忘此，雖聖賢不能化也 - Conditional statement meaning 'If one forgets this, even sages and virtuous men cannot transform them.'
  • 故盜賊之三犯者，只好飲之以醋而已 - Metaphorical statement suggesting that those who repeatedly commit theft or robbery can only be dealt with by 'drinking vinegar,' implying harsh punishment or humiliation.
  • 至殺人者，雖因奸也，實緣讪笑公而頓激忿怒 - Complex sentence explaining that even if someone kills another due to adultery, it is actually because they were mocked in public and suddenly became enraged.
  • 斯人尚有良心 - Statement affirming that such a person still has a sense of conscience.
  • 彼甄別公者，直斯人不若矣 - Comparative statement meaning 'Those who mock others in public are even worse than this person.'
  • 當以醋灌之 - Metaphorical statement suggesting that those who mock others in public should be treated with the same harshness as 'drinking vinegar.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 盜賊之三犯者 - Refers to repeat offenders in theft or robbery, a common theme in classical Chinese legal and moral discourse.
  • 殺人者 - Refers to murderers, often discussed in the context of legal and ethical considerations.
  • 因奸 - Refers to adultery, a significant social and moral issue in classical Chinese society.
  • 讪笑公 - Refers to public mockery or ridicule, which was considered a serious social offense.
  • 醋 - In this context, vinegar symbolizes harsh punishment or humiliation, a metaphorical use in classical Chinese literature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妾何如人，敢当夫人青目耶 - 'What kind of person am I, to deserve the favor of the lady?' A humble and self-deprecating response from the young woman.
  • 妾夫素跟官 - 'My husband has always served as an official,' indicating the young woman's husband has a background in government service.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 觀察色喪神颓 - Describes a state of despair and mental exhaustion, indicating the character is in a deep state of depression.
  • 婦起閉門，投怀而慰解之 - A vivid description of the wife comforting her husband by embracing him, a common idiom in Classical Chinese to express intimate and supportive actions.
  • 始得安于阳台 - "阳台" likely refers to a bed or a place of intimacy, but can also be interpreted as a metaphor for a safe haven or a place of rest.
  • 未几，观察得缺 - "未几" means 'soon after' or 'not long after,' indicating a short period of time.
  • 竟以其司阍 - "竟" means 'ultimately' or 'in the end,' and "司阍" refers to a gatekeeper or a servant.
  • 其夫大权在握，任其播弄，观察无可如何，听之而已 - Describes the situation where the husband has absolute power and can manipulate the situation as he pleases, and the observer can do nothing but accept it.
  • 然能劝其主勉事上官 - "然能" means 'yet can,' and "勉事上官" means to encourage the master to serve his superiors diligently.
  • 未几权廉访 - "未几" again means 'soon after,' and "权廉访" refers to a position of power, specifically a censor or inspector.
  • 其夫招摇撞骗，无所不为 - "招摇撞骗" means to swagger and deceive, and "无所不为" means to do anything.
  • 时有富室身犯大辟，许以数万金出罪，富室知为廉访信使，从之，得赃而遁，富室首于中丞 - Describes a rich man who is accused of a serious crime, is offered a bribe by the husband (who is a censor), and escapes with the money. The rich man then reports the incident to a higher official (中丞).
  • 廉访始知其事，亟传妇严讯之 - "始知其事" means 'finally learned about the matter,' and "亟传妇严讯之" means to urgently summon the wife for a strict interrogation.
  • 妇曰：“是非妾夫也，妾本妓女，彼以重聘雇妾来，认为夫妇，嘱妾勾引主人，若得满载而归，许以半分，妾故愿为之使。然不知其何许人，姓氏亦未确也。” - The wife's statement is a defense, claiming that the man is not her husband, that she is a prostitute hired by him, and that he asked her to seduce the master for a share of the proceeds.
  • 廉访无可奈何，坐此败官 - "无可奈何" means 'helpless' or 'powerless,' and "坐此败官" means to be dismissed from office due to this incident.
  • 后妇之本夫来，又不得不重酬妇而遣之 - "后妇之本夫来" means 'later, the wife's original husband came,' and "重酬妇而遣之" means to heavily reward her and send her away.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 观察 - Likely refers to an official or a person of authority, possibly a local magistrate or a government official.
  • 廉访 - Refers to a censor or an inspector, a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing other officials and ensuring their integrity.
  • 中丞 - Refers to a high-ranking official, often a provincial governor or a central government official.
  • 大辟 - Refers to a capital crime, typically punishable by death.
  • 妓女 - Refers to a prostitute, a common profession in ancient China.
  • 重聘 - Refers to a large dowry or a significant sum of money, often used in marriage contracts.
  • 勾引主人 - Refers to seducing the master, which was a common practice in ancient China where concubines or mistresses were often used to influence or gain favor.
  • 满载而归 - Refers to returning with a full load, metaphorically meaning to gain a lot of wealth or benefits.
  • 半分 - Refers to a share or a portion, typically half of the total.
  • 坐此败官 - Refers to being dismissed from office due to misconduct or corruption.
  • 重酬 - Refers to a substantial reward or compensation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 徐大诳者 - A colloquial term for someone known as a great deceiver or trickster.

  • 得其美称 - Means 'gains a good reputation' or 'is known by a good nickname.'
  • 随居停至吴下贸易 - Means 'accompanies his host to trade in Wu (southern region).'
  • 舟泊太子码头 - Means 'the boat docks at the Prince's Wharf.'
  • 予起货去 - Means 'I will go to collect goods.'
  • 汝留此须慎防之 - Means 'you should stay here and be cautious.'
  • 此处多骗子 - Means 'there are many scammers here.'
  • 勿为所诳 - Means 'do not be deceived by them.'
  • 吾不诳人 - Means 'I do not deceive people.'
  • 人其诳我乎 - Means 'will people deceive me?'
  • 人果行其诳 - Means 'if people indeed deceive others.'
  • 我将诳谁矣 - Means 'who will I deceive then?'
  • 咍 - An interjection expressing surprise or disbelief.
  • 此处骗子，皆鲜衣盛服，充衣冠中人 - Means 'the scammers here all wear fine clothes and pretend to be gentlemen.'
  • 汝若遇之，必逢迎不惶 - Means 'if you meet them, you will surely greet them with great respect.'
  • 恐小巫见大巫，堕其术中而不觉也 - Means 'fearing that a lesser sorcerer will be overwhelmed by a greater one, you will fall into their trap without realizing it.'
  • 请予洋银二牧，我将作虎邱之游 - Means 'please give me two silver dollars, I will go on a trip to Tiger Hill.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 问其价，枚值五六文 - Asks for the price, each piece is worth five or six coins. The idiom '值' (value) indicates monetary worth.
  • 徐置百枚 - '徐' can mean 'slowly' or 'gently,' but here it likely means 'carefully' or 'meticulously.' '置' means 'to place' or 'to put.' Together, it suggests placing a hundred pieces carefully.
  • 又以百余钱买一布囊 - '又以' means 'and then with,' indicating a sequence of actions. '百余钱' means 'a hundred or so coins,' and '买一布囊' means 'bought a cloth bag.'
  • 以棉纸裹锡钱，作两封，纳囊中 - '以棉纸裹锡钱' means 'wrapped the tin coins in cotton paper,' '作两封' means 'made two bundles,' and '纳囊中' means 'put them into the bag.'
  • 负之归舟 - '负' means 'to carry,' and '归舟' means 'return to the boat.' Together, it suggests carrying the bag back to the boat.
  • 易短褐毡冠，作乡人状 - '易' means 'to change' or 'to swap,' '短褐' refers to a simple, coarse garment, and '毡冠' means 'felt hat.' '作乡人状' means 'to appear as a local villager.'
  • 以红纸开绸缎账单，怀之 - '以红纸开' means 'opened with red paper,' '绸缎账单' means 'silk and satin invoice,' and '怀之' means 'carried it in his heart' or 'kept it in his possession.'
  • 持雨盖负囊，游于市 - '持雨盖' means 'carrying an umbrella,' '负囊' means 'carrying the bag,' and '游于市' means 'wandering in the market.'
  • 见古玩铺前，坐一客，衣冠甚丽 - '见古玩铺前' means 'saw in front of an antique shop,' '坐一客' means 'a customer was sitting,' and '衣冠甚丽' means 'dressed very elegantly.'
  • 时正隆冬，披火狐裘，貉冠皮鞋，手持玉嘴银头乌木烟管 - '时正隆冬' means 'it was mid-winter,' '披火狐裘' means 'wearing a red fox fur coat,' '貉冠皮鞋' means 'wearing a mink hat and leather shoes,' and '手持玉嘴银头乌木烟管' means 'holding a jade-tipped, silver-headed ebony smoking pipe.'
  • 徐有触于怀，乃坐对门巨室阶上 - '徐有触于怀' means 'he had a sudden thought or feeling,' and '乃坐对门巨室阶上' means 'he then sat on the steps of a large house opposite.'
  • 出其单与锡洋布目前，以真洋钱颠掷 - '出其单' means 'took out the invoice,' '与锡洋布目前' means 'showing the tin coins and cloth to him,' and '以真洋钱颠掷' means 'flipping the real coins.'
  • 而口作怨词 - '而口作怨词' means 'and muttered complaints.'
  • 正自嗫嚅间，客见其多金，洋洋来前 - '正自嗫嚅间' means 'while he was muttering to himself,' '客见其多金' means 'the customer saw his wealth,' and '洋洋来前' means 'approached him arrogantly.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 古玩铺 - Refers to an antique shop, a common place in traditional Chinese markets.
  • 衣冠甚丽 - Describes someone dressed very elegantly, often associated with the upper class or wealthy individuals.
  • 火狐裘 - A red fox fur coat, a symbol of wealth and status.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因侄女将嫁 - Literally means 'because my niece is going to be married,' introduces the context of the niece's upcoming marriage.
  • 兄与我百洋并账单 - Literally means 'my elder brother gave me a hundred yamens (a unit of currency) and an invoice,' indicates the financial assistance provided by the brother.
  • 使置嫁衣 - Literally means 'to buy wedding clothes,' specifies the purpose of the money and invoice.
  • 不识大绸缎局在何处 - Literally means 'do not know where the large silk and satin shop is,' expresses the protagonist's unfamiliarity with the location.
  • 又不知买单中物敷用否 - Literally means 'also do not know if the items on the invoice are sufficient,' indicates uncertainty about whether the items listed on the invoice will be enough.
  • 无人指点 - Literally means 'no one to guide,' expresses the lack of assistance.
  • 以是怨恨耳 - Literally means 'therefore, I am resentful,' summarizes the protagonist's feelings.
  • 皆奁中急需物 - Literally means 'all are urgently needed items for the dowry,' describes the items on the invoice.
  • 信为真实 - Literally means 'truly believe it to be true,' expresses the guest's confidence in the authenticity of the invoice.
  • 笑指之曰 - Literally means 'laughed and pointed out,' describes the guest's action.
  • 北去里许 - Literally means 'about a mile north,' indicates the direction and distance to the silk and satin shop.
  • 高墙大门内有绸缎局 - Literally means 'inside a high wall and large gate, there is a silk and satin shop,' describes the location of the shop.
  • 是我亲戚所设 - Literally means 'it is set up by my relative,' indicates the relationship between the guest and the shop owner.
  • 两京十八省客 - Literally means 'customers from the two capitals and eighteen provinces,' emphasizes the shop's reputation and clientele.
  • 真大行市也 - Literally means 'truly a major market,' praises the shop's significance.
  • 汝往必无欺 - Literally means 'you will definitely not be deceived,' assures the protagonist.
  • 我目不识丁 - Literally means 'I cannot read or write,' indicates the protagonist's illiteracy.
  • 虽有招牌，奈不能认 - Literally means 'although there is a sign, I cannot recognize it,' expresses the protagonist's inability to read the sign.
  • 请借贵步一引可乎 - Literally means 'please allow me to follow you,' politely requests assistance.
  • 幸我无事，汝其从之 - Literally means 'fortunately, I have nothing to do, so you can follow me,' indicates the guest's willingness to help.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 侄女 - Refers to the protagonist's niece, indicating a familial relationship.

  • 绸缎局 - A silk and satin shop, indicating a place where high-quality fabrics are sold.
  • 亲戚 - A relative, indicating a familial relationship.

  • This clause expresses the protagonist's unfamiliarity with the location of the silk and satin shop and uncertainty about the sufficiency of the items on the invoice.
  • This clause summarizes the protagonist's feelings of frustration due to the lack of assistance.
  • This sequence describes the guest's confidence in the authenticity of the invoice and his action of pointing out the location of the shop.
  • This clause describes the location of the shop and the relationship between the guest and the shop owner.
  • This clause emphasizes the shop's reputation and clientele.
  • This clause reassures the protagonist that he will not be deceived.
  • This clause expresses the protagonist's illiteracy and inability to read the sign.
  • This clause politely requests assistance.
  • This clause indicates the guest's willingness to help.

  • 面尚未至 - Indicates a delay in service, 'The noodles haven't arrived yet.'
  • 我患疟痢未痊，不意又发作矣 - Description of a recurring illness, 'I have malaria and diarrhea that haven't fully healed, and it seems to have flared up again.'

  • 火狐短挂与烟管不过三十余金 - Valuation of the items, 'A short coat and a pipe are worth no more than thirty gold pieces.'

  • 强按于榻污之 - '强按' means to forcibly hold, '于榻' means on the bed, and '污之' means to defile or violate. This describes the bandit's assault.
  • 诸盗掠得财物，齐聚外室 - '诸盗' means the bandits, '掠得财物' means the stolen valuables, and '齐聚外室' means to gather in the outer room. This describes the bandits' actions after the robbery.

  • 噫！汝为此不义事，必遭诛戮 - '噫' is an interjection expressing surprise or disapproval, '汝' means you, '为此不义事' means doing this unjust thing, and '必遭诛戮' means you will surely be punished. This is the bandit's warning.
  • 我等为汝误矣 - '我等' means we, '为汝误' means misled by you, and '矣' is a particle indicating completion. This expresses the bandit's frustration.

  • 汝不记吾党规条乎 - '汝' means you, '不记' means do not remember, '吾党' means our group, and '规条' means rules. This is a reminder of the group's rules.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence '卜完人散，悟知子诣道人膝前，跪称仙师求教' must be read as a single, continuous action: 'After divination, the people dispersed, and Wuzhi came before the Daoist master, knelt, and called him 'Immortal Teacher' to seek instruction.'
  • The pair of sentences '孺子可教哉！惜费聪明于无益之地' forms a contrast: 'The child is teachable, but it is a pity to waste intelligence on something unproductive.'
  • The clause '仙道犹儒道，须专心致志，历阶而进' contrasts the ways of immortality and Confucianism, emphasizing the need for dedication and gradual progress.
  • The final clause '皆好事者托词以误世也' summarizes the Daoist master's critique of false claims about immortality.

Proper Nouns

  • 道人 - The term used to address the Daoist master, but no specific name is given.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 十二司戴元首，合而为身毒国 - Metaphorical structure where '十二司' (twelve departments) is compared to the '元首' (head), and together they form '身毒国' (Shendu Kingdom). '身毒' is a transliteration of 'India', but here it refers to a metaphorical state.
  • 毒译督 - Transliteration or phonetic rendering of a word meaning 'supervise' or 'oversee'.
  • 言一司总督诸司也 - Explains that one department oversees all others, using the metaphor of a head overseeing the body.
  • 耳目口鼻喉舌最贵，比中华之宰相尚书卿贰 - Metaphor comparing the most important organs (ear, eye, mouth, nose, throat, tongue) to high-ranking officials in the Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 左右肱比都督将军 - Metaphor comparing the arms to generals.
  • 心腹比抚按 - Metaphor comparing the heart and abdomen to provincial governors and censors.
  • 臂指比提镇 - Metaphor comparing the arm and finger to military commanders.
  • 前阳主刑名，后阴主钱谷 - Metaphor comparing the front (yang) to the administration of justice and the back (yin) to the management of finances and grain.
  • 左右股比汛弁 - Metaphor comparing the legs to lower-ranking military officers.
  • 皮毛司比佐贰杂职 - Metaphor comparing the skin and hair to minor officials.
  • 自王以下，皆布衣，以色分品级 - Describes a society where everyone, including the king, wears plain clothes, with social status indicated by color.
  • 王衣黊，上司衣朱，中司衣蓝，下司衣青，民衣白 - Specifies the colors of clothing for different ranks.
  • 田地无主，亦无旷土，产香谷，大于中华之稻米，一岁三收 - Describes a land where there is no private ownership, no idle land, and a highly productive crop.
  • 至春夏秋三时，人之有力者，不拘何地，尽量布种 - Describes the practice of planting crops extensively during the spring, summer, and autumn seasons.
  • 诸司督耕，能多得谷者，司举奏王，以下司爵之，有递迁至朱衣者 - Describes a system where officials oversee farming, and those who produce the most grain are rewarded with promotions.
  • 第非考较出身，仅虚职荣之耳 - Indicates that these promotions are not based on merit but are honorary.
  • 怠情者，发穷黎岛，日给一粥终其身 - Describes the punishment for lazy individuals, who are sent to a remote island and given only one bowl of porridge per day for life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 年豐 - Refers to a year of good harvest, indicating abundance.
  • 家藏一歲之用 - Means to store enough food for an entire year, emphasizing preparedness.
  • 餘盡歸元首 - Implies that the surplus is returned to the central authority.
  • 分貯十二司 - Indicates that the surplus is distributed among twelve administrative departments or agencies.
  • 若歉年 - Refers to a year of poor harvest, indicating scarcity.
  • 十二司積儲，仍散於民 - Means that the stored resources in the twelve departments are redistributed to the people.
  • 故無饑馑 - Indicates that there is no famine due to the system.
  • 亦無貧富 - Suggests that there is no extreme poverty or wealth.
  • 上下皆食谷 - Means that both the upper and lower classes eat grain, indicating a uniform diet.
  • 不知有菜，何來荤腥 - Implies that there is no knowledge of vegetables or meat, suggesting a strict vegetarian diet.
  • 雖產有金银珠玉宝贝 - Indicates that although precious metals and jewels are produced, they are not valued.
  • 不足貴也 - Means that these items are not considered valuable.
  • 不過為布地飾屋之需 - Suggests that they are used only for decorating the ground and houses.
  • 狁中华之砖瓦朱粉 - The meaning of '狁' is unclear, but it might be a reference to a specific decorative element similar to Chinese bricks, tiles, and paint.
  • 所贵者米布 - Means that the valuable items are rice and cloth.
  • 如中华之银钱 - Compares these items to Chinese silver coins, indicating their value.
  • 凡动用之物，皆以此交易 - Means that all goods are traded using these items.
  • 我王相传为净饭王分枝八十四世裔孙 - Indicates that the current king is the 84th descendant of King Suddhodana, a reference to the lineage of the Buddha.
  • 其长枝，即阿弥陀佛 - Suggests that the main branch of the lineage is the Buddha Amitabha.
  • 代传一那摩佛 - Indicates that the lineage is passed down through the lineage of the Buddha Namuo.
  • 即中华所称喇嘛大班禅活佛也 - Refers to the title of the Dalai Lama, a significant religious figure in Tibetan Buddhism.
  • 能参四禅八定 - Means that the Dalai Lama can enter the four meditative absorptions and eight trances, indicating advanced spiritual abilities.
  • 诸司选民之后，俊秀者送入学道 - Means that after selecting the people, the talented ones are sent to study the path.
  • 或三四年，或五六年 - Indicates that the duration of study can vary from three to six years.
  • 由那摩佛考较道行之浅深 - Means that the Dalai Lama examines the depth of their spiritual practice.
  • 送王补诸司缺 - Indicates that those who pass are sent to fill positions in the various departments.
  • 故皆慈祥，为国为民 - Suggests that they are all kind and serve the country and the people.

  • 凡女皆习工作 - Means that all women learn to work, indicating a focus on practical skills.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 制軍 - A title for a high-ranking military official, often translated as 'military governor' or 'commander.'
  • 門下皆一時譽髦 - '門下' refers to the retinue or followers of the military governor, and '譽髦' refers to distinguished or talented individuals. Together, this phrase means 'his retinue consisted of the most talented and distinguished individuals of the time.'
  • 風雅甚 - '風雅' refers to a refined or cultured demeanor, and '甚' intensifies it, meaning 'very cultured' or 'highly refined.'
  • 琴棋詩畫各擅一長 - This phrase lists the four arts (music, chess, poetry, and painting) and indicates that each person excels in one of them.
  • 咸使名士傳之 - '咸' means 'all,' and '使名士傳之' means 'caused the literati to record it,' implying that the military governor's retinue recorded or wrote about these activities.
  • 孝廉 - A title given to individuals who were recommended for office based on their virtue and literary talent.
  • 年逾弱冠 - '弱冠' refers to the age of 20, and '年逾' means 'over,' so this phrase means 'over the age of 20.'
  • 詩如其人 - This phrase means 'his poetry reflected his character.'
  • 家屡空 - '家屡空' means 'his family was frequently in poverty' or 'he was often in financial difficulty.'
  • 投制軍謀食 - '謀食' means 'to seek sustenance' or 'to find a way to make a living,' so this phrase means 'he sought support from the military governor.'
  • 寵姬某氏 - '寵姬' refers to a favored concubine, and '某氏' is a placeholder for her name.
  • 詩筆清新 - This phrase means 'her poetry was fresh and clear.'
  • 傳遞诗词 - This phrase means 'they exchanged poems,' implying a romantic correspondence.
  • 文君之約 - '文君' refers to Zhuo Wenjun, a famous courtesan in Chinese history who married Sima Xiangru, a poet. This phrase alludes to a romantic or marriage-like agreement.
  • 逾花圃后垣同逸 - '逾' means 'to leap over,' '花圃' means 'flower garden,' '后垣' means 'rear wall,' and '同逸' means 'to escape together.' This phrase describes how they escaped together by jumping over the rear wall of the garden.
  • 仆妇书童报二人夜不归室之事 - '仆妇书童' refers to servants and pages, and '报二人夜不归室之事' means 'reported that the two people had not returned to their rooms at night.'
  • 迅往南追取二人首 - '迅往南追' means 'swiftly went south to pursue,' and '取二人首' means 'to take the heads of the two people,' implying a violent pursuit.
  • 认姬作汝妻，被孝廉奸拐，故追而斩之 - This phrase means 'acknowledge the concubine as your wife, who was kidnapped by the scholar-official, so pursue and kill them.'
  • 投所在官司自首 - '投所在官司' means 'to surrender to the local authorities,' and '自首' means 'to confess.'

  • 花圃后垣 - The 'flower garden' and 'rear wall' are specific locations within a compound, often associated with private or secluded spaces.
  • 自首 - The act of confessing to authorities, a common legal practice in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 弁领命将行 - 弁领 is likely a title or name, and 命将行 means 'was about to set out on a mission.'
  • 夫人遽出喝止 - 遽出 means 'hurriedly came out,' and 喝止 means 'shouted to stop.'
  • 机事不密则害成 - A classical saying meaning 'if a secret is not kept confidential, it will lead to disaster.'
  • 此等事想已飏播省垣矣 - 飏播 means 'spread widely,' and 省垣 refers to the provincial government or high officials.
  • 引盗入室，斩之非义 - 引盗入室 means 'bringing a thief into the house,' and 斩之非义 means 'killing him would be unjust.'
  • 一顶绿头巾终脱不去 - 绿头巾 (green hat) refers to being cuckolded, implying the consequences of the action.
  • 徒然掩耳盗铃 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to cover one's ears while stealing a bell,' symbolizing self-deception.
  • 不贻笑士林耶 - 贻笑士林 means 'to be ridiculed by the scholarly community.'
  • 孝廉才名甚著 - 孝廉 is a title for a scholar-official, and 才名甚著 means 'his talent and reputation are very prominent.'
  • 爱之者不仅夫君一人 - 爱之者 means 'those who love him,' and 不仅夫君一人 means 'not just my husband alone.'
  • 使士林抱不平，察实而发其覆，贻害无穷矣 - 抱不平 means 'to feel aggrieved,' 察实而发其覆 means 'to investigate and expose the truth,' and 贻害无穷矣 means 'will cause endless harm.'
  • 夫君聪明阔达 - 聪明阔达 means 'wise and broad-minded.'
  • 恐一时忿怒昧之耳 - 恐 means 'fear,' 一时忿怒 means 'a moment of anger,' and 昧之耳 means 'clouded his judgment.'
  • 杨越、裴晋二公，皆人杰也 - 杨越 and 裴晋 are proper names, and 人杰 means 'outstanding individuals.'
  • 盗其侍妾者，不但不追，间有厚赠者 - 盗其侍妾者 means 'those who stole his concubine,' 不但不追 means 'not only did not pursue,' and 间有厚赠者 means 'sometimes even gave generous gifts.'
  • 曷不仿而为之 - 曷不 means 'why not,' and 仿而为之 means 'follow their example.'
  • 请作札遣家人赠以行资 - 请作札 means 'please write a letter,' 遣家人 means 'send family members,' and 赠以行资 means 'give travel funds.'
  • 孝廉有不感愧图报者乎 - 感愧 means 'feel ashamed,' and 图报 means 'repay.'
  • 且以美姬赠名士 - 美姬 means 'beautiful concubine,' and 名士 means 'famous scholar.'
  • 孰得孰失，君试思之 - 孰得孰失 means 'what is gained and what is lost,' and 君试思之 means 'you should think about it.'
