  • 制军 - A title for a military commander or general.
  • 非夫人之论，我几大误 - Classical expression meaning 'If not for your lady's opinion, I would have made a great mistake.'
  • 花甲 - Classical term for the age of sixty.
  • 少女 - Can mean 'young girl' or 'daughter,' and in classical usage may imply a daughter of marriageable age.
  • 造物亦几费经营 - 'The heavens (or nature) must have spent considerable effort in creating her.' '造物' refers to the creator or nature; '费经营' is a metaphor for effort or toil.
  • 莽夫 - A term for a rough, unrefined man.
  • 罗而致之 - 'To gather her and bring her to me.' The verb '罗' can mean to gather or capture, often with a connotation of careful selection.
  • 姿质 - Means natural endowments or talents.
  • 岂非天下大快事耶 - 'Is this not a great joy for the world?' A rhetorical question expressing strong approval.
  • 所不预言者 - 'Which I did not previously declare,' or 'which I did not announce beforehand.'
  • 学未大成 - Classical expression for incomplete or unfinished education.
  • 欲其精进而后成之 - 'I desired her to advance further before completing her education.'
  • 好逑 - From the *Book of Poetry*, meaning 'a good match' or 'a suitable partner.'
  • 妙手空空 - 'Empty-handed' or 'without any possessions.'
  • 腰缠何藉 - 'What use is the money tied around one’s waist?' A reference to carrying money (often in a belt or sash), used metaphorically for financial support.
  • 累及 - Means to burden or involve.
  • 馈赆 - A parting gift, often given when a woman is sent away in marriage or when someone departs.
  • 伏冀哂存 - 'I humbly hope you will accept it with a smile.' '伏冀' is a humble expression of hope; '哂存' means to accept or keep with a smile.
  • 捡点 - Means to check or prepare carefully.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 制军 - A classical title for a military commander or general, often used in the context of military governance or high-ranking officials.
  • 花甲 - A term for the age of 60, based on the traditional Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 巫山神女 - A reference to the legendary goddess of Mount Wu (Wushan), from the *Chu Ci* (Songs of Chu), who is said to have fallen in love with the poet and statesman Qu Yuan (King Chu). This is a well-known literary and romantic allusion.
  • 好逑 - A term from the *Book of Poetry*, used in the famous line '窈窕淑女，君子好逑' (The graceful and virtuous lady is the gentleman’s ideal match). It is a classical expression of a proper and ideal marriage.
  • 馈赆 - A parting gift, often given when a woman is sent away in marriage or when someone departs. It is a traditional custom in classical Chinese society.
  • 腰缠 - A reference to the traditional practice of carrying money in a sash or belt, often used metaphorically to indicate wealth or financial support.
  • 下走 - A humble term for a subordinate or servant, used in classical Chinese to show deference.

  • 巫山神女 - A mythological figure, not a historical person.
  • 楚王 - A title, not a specific historical figure (could refer to King Chu, such as Qu Yuan).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 二人得书，惭愧悲感，望东叩首 - Formal expression describing the emotional reaction and gesture of reverence; '望东叩首' (bowing eastward) may symbolize respect or submission.
  • 夫子之德竟是二天 - Hyperbolic expression praising the teacher's virtue as equivalent to two heavens.
  • 当作犬马图报 - Humble expression of gratitude, meaning 'to repay like a dog or horse' (i.e., to serve loyally).
  • 再拜作禀，申意其词 - Formal language indicating a second bow and a written expression of intent or feelings.
  • 忆自亥子春秋，获游夫子门下 - Reference to a specific period ('亥子春秋') and the privilege of studying under the teacher.
  • 巴渝妄奏，得近云门 - Metaphorical language suggesting an opportunity to approach the teacher's knowledge or grace.
  • 菉葹下材，顿亲琪树 - Metaphor comparing oneself to lowly grass and suddenly being close to a precious tree (symbolizing the teacher).
  • 康成雅度，欲听慧婢之诗 - Refers to the teacher's (康成, Kong Rong) noble demeanor and willingness to listen to the student's poetry.
  • 小子何知，俾作丽娘之傅 - Modest statement about the student's lack of knowledge and the teacher's guidance.
  • 银蝉入户，共赓隔巷之清歌 - Metaphor for the student's entry into the teacher's world and participation in shared cultural activities.
  • 红杏出墙，愿借邻家以艳色 - Metaphor for the student's desire to learn from the teacher's knowledge.
  • 宓妃留枕，非关韩豫偷香 - Metaphorical reference to a romantic or intimate relationship, but clarified to be unrelated to illicit behavior.
  • 携手星奔，抱衾露宿 - Describes a close, possibly romantic relationship.
  • 方虞飞洞宾之剑，何期添裴晋之装 - Metaphorical reference to unexpected changes or additions in the relationship.
  • 夫子休休有容，勤勤乐育 - Praises the teacher's broad-mindedness and dedication to teaching.
  • 使狗偷鼠窃，不作青磷 - Metaphorical statement about the teacher's ability to elevate even lowly individuals.
  • 成凤侣鸾俦，同偕白首 - Metaphor for a harmonious and lasting relationship.
  • 高以赆仪四百，行李增光 - Refers to a generous gift (赆仪) and its symbolic value.
  • 从此弱水三千，片帆稳渡 - Metaphor for overcoming difficulties with ease.
  • 感周旋之仔细，益愧寸衷 - Expresses gratitude and humility for the teacher's careful guidance.
  • 期报答以辛勤，难酬尺泽 - Statement about the difficulty of repaying the teacher's efforts.
  • 惟思砺顽钝之器，庶不贻冰鉴之羞 - Resolution to improve oneself to avoid shame.
  • 千载遭逢，万言莫罄 - Expression of the depth of gratitude and the inadequacy of words to convey it.
  • 敢因臂指，敬布腹心 - Formal expression of loyalty and sincerity.
  • 孝廉捷南宫，由词林入谏垣 - Refers to a successful career advancement from the literary academy to the advisory council.
  • 有与制军为难者，多方营救之 - Description of the teacher's efforts to help someone in trouble with the military governor.
  • 与某姬偕老且多子云 - Indicates a long and fruitful marriage with a wife.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夫子 - A respectful term for a teacher or mentor.
  • 康成 - Refers to Kong Rong (153–208), a scholar and official of the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 银蝉 - A metaphor for the teacher's knowledge or grace.
  • 红杏出墙 - A metaphor for seeking knowledge or learning from others.
  • 宓妃留枕 - Refers to the myth of the goddess of the Luo River, symbolizing a romantic or intimate relationship.
  • 飞洞宾之剑 - Refers to the legendary swordsman Lu Dongbin, symbolizing unexpected challenges or changes.
  • 裴晋之装 - Refers to the legendary swordsman Pei Xun, symbolizing unexpected additions or changes.
  • 孝廉 - A scholar-official selected through the imperial examination system.
  • 词林 - Refers to the literary academy or the world of literature.
  • 谏垣 - Refers to the advisory council or the role of a censor.
  • 制军 - Refers to the military governor or a high-ranking military official.

Proper Nouns

  • 康成 - Kong Rong (153–208), a scholar and official of the Eastern Han dynasty.

[471]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生而警敏多机智 - Describes Ke Liangyu's character traits: 'born with alertness, quick-witted, and full of intelligence.'
  • 多方构之不得 - Means 'various methods were used to investigate but nothing was found,' indicating an unsuccessful investigation.
  • 米具所为也 - 'Must be the work of Mi Ju,' where '米具' (mǐ jù) is a person's name, and '所为' (suǒ wéi) means 'the work of' or 'done by.'
  • 日者潜窥汝室 - 'The day before, someone secretly peeped into your room,' where '日者' (rì zhě) can mean 'the day before' or 'a certain day.'
  • 具即与耳语而去 - 'Mi Ju then whispered to him and left,' where '耳语' (ěr yǔ) means 'whispered.'
  • 缉之果得 - 'Investigating this indeed led to the discovery,' where '缉' (jī) means 'investigate' or 'track down.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦良玉 - Ke Liangyu was a renowned female general and leader of the Shizhu Tusi (Stone Pillar Tusi) during the Ming dynasty. She is celebrated for her military prowess and loyalty to the Ming court.
  • 石柱土司 - The Shizhu Tusi was a local autonomous region in Sichuan province, governed by a hereditary leader known as a Tusi. The region was part of the Ming dynasty's system of indirect rule over minority areas.
  • 邻人被窃 - This refers to a common occurrence in rural or semi-autonomous regions where theft was a frequent problem, often leading to community-wide efforts to solve it.

Proper Nouns

  • 秦良玉 - Ke Liangyu, a historical figure.
  • 石柱土司 - The Shizhu Tusi, a local autonomous region.
  • 米具 - Mi Ju, a person's name.

[472]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 及笄 - A term referring to the coming-of-age ceremony for girls, typically at age 15 in ancient China.
  • 自择夫 - Literally means 'choose their own husbands,' indicating a cultural practice where young women chose their spouses.
  • 纵少男女于山野 - A descriptive phrase meaning 'allow young men and women to gather in the mountains and fields,' suggesting a traditional matchmaking event.
  • 唱歌求配 - Means 'sing songs to find a partner,' describing a method of courtship through singing.
  • 年及冠 - Refers to reaching the age of 20, the traditional age of adulthood for men.
  • 无父母昆季 - Means 'without parents or siblings,' indicating a lack of immediate family support.
  • 贫而好学 - Describes someone who is poor but loves learning.
  • 美秀而文 - Means 'beautiful, elegant, and cultured,' describing a person's appearance and character.
  • 君得我不忧不富贵 - A phrase expressing confidence that the speaker will bring wealth and status to her partner.
  • 我得君不忧不多闻 - Similarly, expresses confidence that the speaker will benefit from her partner's knowledge and experiences.
  • 事在人为 - Means 'success depends on human effort,' emphasizing the importance of personal initiative.
  • 家贫易为力 - Means 'it's easier to help with family poverty,' indicating a willingness to assist with financial difficulties.
  • 腹贫须好学 - Means 'mental poverty requires good learning,' suggesting that intellectual growth is necessary to overcome inner poverty.
  • 君所感者家贫 - Means 'what you feel troubled by is family poverty,' acknowledging the partner's concerns.
  • 我所感者腹贫 - Means 'what I feel troubled by is mental poverty,' acknowledging the speaker's own concerns.
  • 相得甚欢 - Means 'they got along very well,' describing a harmonious relationship.
  • 恒执女工伴读 - Means 'always doing needlework while studying,' describing a dual activity of work and study.
  • 忽谓生曰 - Means 'suddenly said to the man,' indicating a spontaneous moment of communication.
  • 曷求富强之学 - Means 'why not seek learning about wealth and strength,' suggesting a practical approach to personal and societal improvement.
  • 闭户讲论 - Means 'closed the door and discussed,' describing a focused and private study session.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 及笄 - A significant rite of passage for girls in ancient China, marking their transition to adulthood.
  • 自择夫 - Reflects a cultural practice where young women had a degree of autonomy in choosing their partners.
  • 纵少男女于山野 - Describes a traditional matchmaking event where young people would gather in natural settings to meet potential partners.
  • 唱歌求配 - A method of courtship that involved singing songs to express interest and attract a partner.
  • 年及冠 - Marks the age of adulthood for men in ancient China, often associated with taking on adult responsibilities.
  • 无父母昆季 - Indicates a lack of immediate family support, which could have significant social and economic implications.
  • 贫而好学 - Highlights the value placed on education and self-improvement, even in the face of poverty.
  • 美秀而文 - Describes a person who is not only physically attractive but also refined and cultured, reflecting the ideal of a well-rounded individual.
  • 君得我不忧不富贵 - Reflects the mutual benefits and expectations in a marriage, where both partners bring valuable qualities to the relationship.
  • 我得君不忧不多闻 - Similarly, emphasizes the mutual enrichment that comes from a partnership.
  • 事在人为 - A common Confucian belief that success is achieved through human effort and perseverance.
  • 家贫易为力 - Suggests that practical support, such as financial assistance, is easier to provide and more tangible.
  • 腹贫须好学 - Emphasizes the importance of intellectual growth and education in overcoming inner poverty.
  • 君所感者家贫 - Acknowledges the partner's concerns about financial stability.
  • 我所感者腹贫 - Acknowledges the speaker's own concerns about intellectual and personal development.
  • 相得甚欢 - Describes a harmonious and happy relationship, reflecting the ideal of a strong partnership.
  • 恒执女工伴读 - Describes a dual activity of work and study, highlighting the balance between practical skills and intellectual pursuits.
  • 忽谓生曰 - Indicates a spontaneous moment of communication, suggesting a natural and genuine interaction.
  • 曷求富强之学 - Suggests a practical and forward-thinking approach to personal and societal improvement.
  • 闭户讲论 - Describes a focused and private study session, emphasizing the importance of dedicated learning.

Proper Nouns

  • 及笄 - The coming-of-age ceremony for girls.
  • 土俗 - Local customs.
  • 春秋之际 - The period of spring and autumn.
  • 马生 - The name of the man, Ma Sheng.
  • 土司宗族 - A term referring to a local ruler's clan.
  • 年及冠 - The age of 20, the traditional age of adulthood for men.
  • 无父母昆季 - Without parents or siblings.
  • 贫而好学 - Poor but loves learning.
  • 美秀而文 - Beautiful, elegant, and cultured.
  • 玉 - The name of the woman, Yu.
  • 父母及兄 - Parents and brothers.
  • 家贫易为力 - Easier to help with family poverty.
  • 腹贫须好学 - Mental poverty requires good learning.
  • 《韬钤武备》 - A book on military strategy.
  • 《三农致富》 - A book on agricultural prosperity.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 得之矣 - Means 'I have it now' or 'I have found it.'
  • 遍历荒山 - Means 'to traverse the wilderness mountains.'
  • 得无主之地数十顷 - Means 'acquired unowned land for dozens of acres.'
  • 归而尽其所有皆易钱 - Means 'returned and exchanged all his possessions for money.'
  • 不足，乞贷父兄亲族以益之 - Means 'when it was insufficient, he borrowed from his relatives to make up for it.'
  • 使生置芋粟，一名包谷 - Means 'he instructed his servants to plant sweet potatoes and corn, also known as corn.'
  • 此贱而易成之物 - Means 'these are cheap and easy-to-grow crops.'
  • 遍撤山地 - Means 'he cleared the mountain land.'
  • 日游里闾，结好众瑶妇 - Means 'he daily roamed the village and befriended many瑶women.'
  • 得其爱戴心 - Means 'gained their affection and respect.'
  • 谓之曰 - Means 'told them.'
  • 本年当大旱 - Means 'this year will be a drought.'
  • 救荒之计我已密布山间 - Means 'my plan to alleviate the famine is already in place in the mountains.'
  • 将来成熟时可以周济汝等 - Means 'when they mature, they can help you.'
  • 但须为我照料耳 - Means 'but you must take care of them.'
  • 众妇皆悦 - Means 'the women were all pleased.'
  • 为之挟刃巡逻 - Means 'they patrolled with weapons.'
  • 秀实时争为收割 - Means 'when the crops were ready, they competed to harvest them.'
  • 不失一茎 - Means 'not a single stem was missed.'
  • 时果夏秋无雨 - Means 'indeed, there was no rain in summer and autumn.'
  • 禾苗枯槁 - Means 'the crops withered.'
  • 惟此独茂 - Means 'only these crops thrived.'
  • 计口授瑶妇粟 - Means 'he distributed rice according to the number of people.'
  • 欢呼拜谢而去 - Means 'they left after bowing and thanking him.'
  • 尚余千钟 - Means 'there were still thousands of measures left.'
  • 粜之得千余金 - Means 'selling them earned him over a thousand gold coins.'
  • 偿债之外，犹称小康 - Means 'after paying off debts, he still lived a comfortable life.'
  • 次年，瑶妇皆来请种 - Means 'the next year, the瑶women all came to request planting.'
  • 愿为耕耘 - Means 'they wanted to cultivate the land.'
  • 今年应涝 - Means 'this year should be a flood.'
  • 惟稷独成 - Means 'only millet will succeed.'
  • 购种遍播之 - Means 'he bought seeds and sowed them everywhere.'
  • 夏秋果大雨 - Means 'indeed, there was heavy rain in summer and autumn.'
  • 诸谷皆淹没 - Means 'all other crops were submerged.'
  • 稷高丈余，不畏水 - Means 'millet grew over ten feet tall and was not afraid of water.'
  • 又获丰收 - Means 'and had another bountiful harvest.'
  • 仍分给酬劳 - Means 'he still distributed rewards.'
  • 外剩数千钟 - Means 'there were still thousands of measures left.'
  • 粜之大富 - Means 'selling them made him very wealthy.'
  • 起厦屋居之 - Means 'he built large houses to live in.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 瑶妇 - Refers to瑶women, a term that may indicate ethnic or cultural identity.
  • 芋粟，一名包谷 - Refers to sweet potatoes and corn, which are common crops in Chinese agriculture.
  • 夏秋无雨 - Refers to a drought in summer and autumn, a common agricultural concern.
  • 禾苗枯槁 - Describes the state of crops during a drought.
  • 稷 - Refers to millet, an important grain in Chinese agriculture.
  • 厦屋 - Refers to large houses, indicating prosperity.

Proper Nouns

  • 玉 - A proper name, possibly referring to a person named '玉' (Jade).
  • 瑶妇 - A term referring to瑶women, possibly indicating an ethnic or cultural group.
  • 芋粟，一名包谷 - Common crops in Chinese agriculture.
  • 稷 - An important grain in Chinese agriculture.
  • 厦屋 - Refers to large houses, indicating prosperity.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 察官民皆乏食 - Means 'observed that both officials and the common people were suffering from food shortages.'
  • 流为盗贼 - Means 'became thieves and bandits,' indicating the social unrest caused by famine.
  • 石柱赖以无恙 - Means 'the Shizhu area remained unharmed,' suggesting that the area was relatively stable compared to others.
  • 男女咸敬佩良玉如神明 - Means 'both men and women revered Liang Yu as if he were a god,' emphasizing his high status and the respect he commanded.
  • 愿为服役者甚众 - Means 'many were willing to serve him,' indicating widespread support.
  • 择其勤能者留之 - Means 'he selected the diligent and capable ones to keep,' showing his method of choosing helpers.
  • 卿何以知天支 - Means 'how do you know the heavenly signs,' referring to Liang Yu's ability to predict weather.
  • 测之皆验 - Means 'all your predictions were accurate,' confirming his skill.
  • 巢居知风，穴居知雨 - Means 'those who live in nests know the wind, and those who live in burrows know the rain,' a metaphor for observing natural signs.
  • 师蚁耳 - Means 'I follow the ants,' indicating his method of predicting weather.
  • 凡旱年其穴必深 - Means 'in dry years, their burrows are deeper,' a sign of drought.
  • 涝年必迁高处 - Means 'in wet years, they move to higher ground,' a sign of flood.
  • 百不失一 - Means 'not once in a hundred times wrong,' indicating high accuracy.
  • 何须高谈天文 - Means 'why bother with grand talk of astronomy,' suggesting simplicity over complexity.
  • 应奇惊异哉 - Means 'or amaze people with strange predictions,' indicating a preference for practicality.
  • 卿言富，则果富矣 - Means 'if you say you are rich, then you are indeed rich,' acknowledging Liang Yu's wealth.
  • 贵乌在 - Means 'where is the nobility,' questioning his social status.
  • 勿急，我后必貤封君 - Means 'do not be in a hurry, I will be ennobled in the future,' indicating a promise of future honor.
  • 为天下妇人吐气 - Means 'to give voice to all women in the world,' suggesting a broader impact on society.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 石柱 - Refers to the Shizhu area, a region known for its stability during times of turmoil.
  • 良玉 - Refers to Liang Yu, a historical figure known for his wisdom and leadership.
  • 巢居知风，穴居知雨 - A classical metaphor for understanding natural signs, often used in texts about divination and weather prediction.
  • 师蚁耳 - A reference to the practice of observing ants to predict weather, a method that was believed to be effective in ancient times.
  • 貤封君 - A term referring to the posthumous ennoblement of a person, often granted to those who have made significant contributions to society.

Proper Nouns

  • 石柱 - The Shizhu area.
  • 良玉 - Liang Yu, a historical figure.
  • 巢居 - Those who live in nests.
  • 穴居 - Those who live in burrows.
  • 貤封君 - Posthumous ennoblement.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 但目下大忧将至，奈何？ - Rhetorical question expressing concern about an impending crisis.
  • 方安居乐业，何出此言？ - Response indicating disbelief or surprise at the mention of a crisis.
  • 富者盗之饵 - Metaphor suggesting that wealth attracts thieves.
  • 邻寨饥荒，有不觊觎我哉？ - Rhetorical question implying that neighboring regions in famine would envy or covet the speaker's resources.
  • 司官素懦弱，又不知训练 - Describes the weakness and lack of preparedness of the local officials.
  • 猝遭强暴，鲜不倾覆 - Statement predicting disaster if the region is suddenly attacked by a strong force.
  • 君速购铜铁木石，觅巧匠 - Imperative urging quick action to prepare defenses.
  • 我将仿诸葛法制连弩，傅以见血封喉之药 - Plan to imitate Zhuge Liang's tactics for defense, using poisoned crossbows.
  • 作救急计 - Plan to prepare for emergencies.
  • 于是作劲弩千张伏垣外 - Describes the preparation of crossbows hidden outside the wall.
  • 夜则埋机当路 - Describes the setup of traps on the road at night.
  • 凡藏粟帛之处，半穴其地作窖 - Describes the process of digging half of the storage areas into the ground to create hidden chambers.
  • 中布铁蒺藜，亦傅毒药 - Describes the placement of iron spikes coated with poison inside the hidden chambers.
  • 上以板覆之 - Describes covering the hidden chambers with boards.
  • 内室财帛皆露，板皆作机，自行无碍 - Describes the setup of the inner room where valuables are visible, and the boards are rigged to flip automatically when stepped on.
  • 人踏之，触机翻转，颠入窖，着蒺藜立死 - Describes the mechanism by which the traps work, causing anyone who steps on them to fall into the hidden chambers and die from the spikes and poison.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 诸葛 - Refers to Zhuge Liang, a famous military strategist and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 见血封喉之药 - Refers to a type of poison that is highly toxic and can cause death quickly.
  • 连弩 - Refers to a type of crossbow that can fire multiple arrows in quick succession, a tactical innovation attributed to Zhuge Liang.
  • 铁蒺藜 - Refers to iron spikes used as a defensive weapon, often placed in hidden locations to injure or kill intruders.

Proper Nouns

  • 诸葛 - Zhuge Liang, a famous military strategist and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 见血封喉之药 - A type of poison that is highly toxic and can cause death quickly.
  • 连弩 - A type of crossbow that can fire multiple arrows in quick succession, a tactical innovation attributed to Zhuge Liang.
  • 铁蒺藜 - Iron spikes used as a defensive weapon, often placed in hidden locations to injure or kill intruders.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 略其地方三百余里 - Literal expression meaning 'to survey an area of three hundred miles.' Emphasizes the scale of the territory being assessed.
  • 料其民得十万余众 - Literal expression meaning 'to estimate the population at over one hundred thousand.' Highlights the size of the population.
  • 环作小寨 - Literal expression meaning 'to encircle and build small camps.' Describes the strategic layout of the camps.
  • 训以兵法，坐作进退，井井有条 - Literal expression meaning 'to train with military tactics, sit, stand, advance, and retreat in an orderly manner.' Emphasizes the disciplined training.
  • 掘长壕周匝，壕中皆置毒蒺藜，上覆机板，并如居室法，外伏毒弩 - Literal expression meaning 'to dig a long trench around the camp, placing poisonous barbed wire in the trench, covering it with movable boards, and setting up poisoned crossbows outside.' Describes the defensive measures.
  • 使能书者居其上，以长绳悬铃达内营，名曰天观，一有所见，即书条摇铃索报信 - Literal expression meaning 'to have someone skilled in writing stationed there, with a long rope and bell extending to the inner camp, called 'Heavenly Observation,' and to write down any sightings and shake the bell to report them.' Describes the communication method.
  • 掘地三四丈，埋瓮，使耳聪者卧其上，能闻百十里人言马嘶 - Literal expression meaning 'to dig a pit three to four zhang deep, burying jars, and have someone with keen hearing lying on top, able to hear people and horses talking and neighing a hundred li away.' Describes the listening system.
  • 广布细作 - Literal expression meaning 'to widely deploy spies.' Describes the intelligence network.
  • 连翔阵，人各执喷筒，以毒药煮细沙，晾透纳筒中 - Literal expression meaning 'to form a flying formation, each person holding a spray tube, filled with finely ground sand cooked with poison, dried, and placed inside the tube.' Describes the special formation and weapon.
  • 每伍间弩手一排护之 - Literal expression meaning 'to have a row of crossbowmen guarding every five people.' Describes the defensive arrangement.
  • 抢上风，顺风扬沙，入人目即迷，疼不得开 - Literal expression meaning 'to seize the upwind position, scatter sand in the wind, causing people to be blinded and in pain, unable to open their eyes.' Describes the tactic used in battle.
  • 杀瞽目也，故易胜 - Literal expression meaning 'to kill the blind, making victory easy.' Describes the effectiveness of the tactic.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 略其地方三百余里 - Refers to the practice of surveying and mapping large territories, a common task in ancient military and administrative contexts.
  • 料其民得十万余众 - Refers to the practice of estimating population sizes, often done for tax and military purposes.
  • 环作小寨 - Refers to the strategic placement of smaller camps to protect a larger central camp, a common military tactic.
  • 训以兵法，坐作进退，井井有条 - Refers to the rigorous training of soldiers in military tactics, emphasizing discipline and order.
  • 掘长壕周匝，壕中皆置毒蒺藜，上覆机板，并如居室法，外伏毒弩 - Describes advanced defensive measures, including trenches, barbed wire, and poisoned crossbows, reflecting the sophistication of ancient military engineering.
  • 使能书者居其上，以长绳悬铃达内营，名曰天观，一有所见，即书条摇铃索报信 - Describes a sophisticated communication system using telescopes and bells, reflecting the importance of intelligence gathering in ancient warfare.
  • 掘地三四丈，埋瓮，使耳聪者卧其上，能闻百十里人言马嘶 - Describes an early form of acoustic surveillance, reflecting the importance of listening posts in ancient military strategy.

  • 杀瞽目也，故易胜 - Refers to the psychological and tactical advantage of blinding the enemy, making them easier to defeat.

  • The phrase '略其地方三百余里，料其民得十万余众' is a compact statement that must be read as a single unit, describing both the survey of the territory and the estimation of the population.

  • The phrase '连翔阵，人各执喷筒，以毒药煮细沙，晾透纳筒中' describes the special formation and weapon, which must be read as a single unit to understand the tactical innovation.
  • The phrase '每伍间弩手一排护之' describes the defensive arrangement, which must be read as a single unit to understand the tactical setup.
  • The phrase '抢上风，顺风扬沙，入人目即迷，疼不得开' describes the battlefield tactic, which must be read as a single unit to understand the method of disorienting the enemy.
  • The phrase '杀瞽目也，故易胜' describes the psychological and tactical advantage, which must be read as a single unit to understand the effectiveness of the tactic.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卿何预知之 - Means 'how did you know beforehand?'
  • 得天观地听力也 - Compound phrase meaning 'by observing the heavens, the earth, and listening to the sounds.'
  • 牲畜在前，土车随后 - Means 'livestock in front, earth carts following,' describing the formation of the bandits.
  • 早以见闻得报 - Means 'early on, through observation and hearing, we received the news.'
  • 我知此法前寨必破 - Means 'I knew that this method would surely break through the front camp.'
  • 故退伏于外，而以地雷火炮伏寨中 - Means 'therefore, I retreated and lay in ambush outside, and set up landmines and fire bombs inside the camp.'
  • 彼见寨多粮食，谅必停留，不知中藏火箭火球等物也 - Means 'they saw that the camp had plenty of grain and must have stayed, not knowing that it contained fire arrows and fireballs.'
  • 彼方住歇，我使人由地道燃火线轰击 - Means 'they just settled down to rest, and I sent people through underground tunnels to ignite the fuse and attack.'
  • 贼已胆裂，外又促之，进退不得，有不降哉 - Means 'the bandits were already terrified, and from outside, they were pushed further, unable to advance or retreat. How could they not surrender?'
  • 今以降人居外屯田，有益无损，是以日见富强 - Means 'now, by having the surrendered bandits farm outside the camp, there is benefit without harm, therefore, we see daily growth in wealth and strength.'
  • 今富贵全矣，卿诚天人也 - Means 'now, wealth and status are complete. You are truly a divine being.'
  • 富不过百万，贵不过土司 - Means 'wealth does not exceed a million, and status does not exceed that of a local chieftain.'
  • 卑卑者何足道哉 - Means 'such humble things are not worth mentioning.'
  • 行将建大功于国家，膺天朝之高爵厚禄，方吐英雄之气耳 - Means 'soon, great achievements will be made for the country, receiving high rank and generous rewards from the imperial court, only then will one truly express heroic spirit.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拔寨俱起 - Means 'marched their entire camp,' indicating a full-scale mobilization.
  • 顺风扬沙 - Describes a military tactic where soldiers throw sand to obscure the enemy's vision.

  • 京师解严 - Means 'the capital was relieved from danger,' indicating the lifting of a siege.
  • 召良玉入觐 - Means 'summoned Lady Liang to the court,' indicating a formal audience with the emperor.

  • 玉辞曰 - Means 'Lady Liang declined,' indicating her refusal of the honor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 开目见本生父母皆喜曰男也 - Narrative phrase meaning 'When he opened his eyes, his birth parents were all happy and said, 'It's a boy!'' '开目见本生' (when he opened his eyes) is a common way to describe the moment of birth.
  • 生觉顶心虚怯不能言，惟笑啼而已 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'When he was born, he felt a void in his mind, unable to speak, only able to laugh and cry.' '顶心虚怯' (void in the mind) is a metaphorical expression for a lack of awareness or understanding.
  • 得乳甘美果腹 - Phrase meaning 'He received sweet milk and was satisfied.' '果腹' (to satisfy hunger) is a common idiom.
  • 至三岁，囱门合而能言 - Phrase meaning 'By the age of three, his fontanelle closed, and he could speak.' '囱门合' (fontanelle closed) is a medical term indicating the closure of the soft spot on a baby's head.
  • 忆前事了了，如在目前 - Phrase meaning 'He remembered past events clearly, as if they were happening right now.' '了了' (clearly) is an idiomatic expression for clarity.
  • 知鬼神敬孝子，是以一举一动无不曲体亲心 - Phrase meaning 'He knew that ghosts and spirits revere filial sons, so every move and action was always in line with his parents' wishes.' '曲体亲心' (in line with his parents' wishes) is an idiomatic expression for filial piety.
  • 父母爱如珍宝 - Phrase meaning 'His parents loved him like a treasure.' '爱如珍宝' (loved like a treasure) is a common idiom.
  • 前生所读书不忘只字 - Phrase meaning 'He remembered every word of what he had read in his past life.' '不忘只字' (remembered every word) is an idiomatic expression for thorough memory.
  • 五六岁即理前业，能诗文，称神童 - Phrase meaning 'At the age of five or six, he could manage his previous studies, write poetry and prose, and was called a prodigy.' '称神童' (called a prodigy) is a common idiom.
  • 八岁入学，十三举于乡，十七成进士，授礼部主事 - Sequence of events meaning 'At eight years old, he entered school; at thirteen, he was recommended by his hometown; at seventeen, he became a Jinshi (imperial scholar), and was appointed as an official in the Ministry of Rites.' '举于乡' (recommended by his hometown) and '授礼部主事' (appointed as an official in the Ministry of Rites) are formal terms.
  • 父母相继殁，哀毁尽礼，守制三年 - Phrase meaning 'His parents died one after another, and he mourned deeply, observing all the rituals, and observed mourning for three years.' '哀毁尽礼' (mourned deeply, observing all the rituals) is a formal term.
  • 二十而娶，入都复职 - Phrase meaning 'At twenty, he married, and returned to the capital to resume his duties.' '复职' (resume his duties) is a formal term.
  • 元日大朝，随班礼毕，皇上退殿 - Phrase meaning 'On New Year's Day, during the grand court ceremony, he followed the ranks in the ritual, and the emperor left the throne.' '随班礼毕' (followed the ranks in the ritual) and '皇上退殿' (the emperor left the throne) are formal terms.
  • 某亲王有命，请主政公今夜入府有话 - Phrase meaning 'A certain prince has a message, asking the Minister of State to come to his mansion tonight for a conversation.' '主政公' (Minister of State) is a formal title.
  • 持此以达，门官即不敢阻，得速见也 - Phrase meaning 'Hold this token to reach him; the gatekeeper will not dare to obstruct you, and you will be able to see him quickly.' '持此以达' (hold this token to reach him) and '得速见也' (you will be able to see him quickly) are formal terms.

Although you have now entered the ranks of officials, worldly affairs are so chaotic, not as enjoyable as the joy of cultivation. Idiomatic Expressions

  • 痘疮者，父母欲火所结 - Metaphorical expression where '欲火' (desire fire) refers to the sexual desires of the parents, which are said to be the cause of the smallpox.
  • 天一生水 - Classical concept from the *Book of Changes* (易经) that refers to the primacy of water in the creation of life.
  • 五脏肾先成 - Indicates that the kidneys form first among the five organs, reflecting the importance of water in the body.
  • 火毒伏其中 - Describes the hidden fire toxicity within the kidneys.
  • 感时气即发 - Means that the disease is triggered by seasonal changes or environmental factors.
  • 平险者 - Refers to those who are less affected by the disease.
  • 清心寡欲 - Means to have a clear mind and few desires, implying a state of purity and simplicity.
  • 茹荤酒 - Refers to consuming meat and alcohol, which are considered impure in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 毒轻易出 - Means that the toxins are easily expelled.
  • 沉醉 - Means to be drunk, indicating excessive consumption of alcohol.
  • 毒必重 - Means that the toxins are more severe.
  • 富贵之儿，殇于痘者多 - Indicates that wealthy children are more likely to die from smallpox.
  • 贫贱之子，伤于痘者少 - Indicates that poor children are less likely to be severely affected by smallpox.
  • 成丁婚配后出痘，成者甚鲜 - Means that those who have reached adulthood and married are less likely to recover from smallpox.
  • 先天巳伤，不能通达故也 - Means that the inherent weakness is too severe to overcome.
  • 凡医皆执此说 - Means that all doctors adhere to this belief.
  • 乡人黄大者，年二十，父母已故，娶有妻室 - Describes a man named Huang Da who is 20 years old, has lost his parents, and is married.
  • 幼未出痘 - Means that he had not contracted smallpox as a child.
  • 是年春间痘症盛行 - Indicates that smallpox was widespread in the spring of that year.
  • 忽患大热，一身尽疼，入于骨骸，求死不得 - Describes the sudden onset of high fever, body pain, and severe discomfort.
  • 七八日间，面现红尖数点 - Describes the appearance of smallpox lesions on the face.
  • 此痘疮也，甚险 - Means that this is smallpox, and it is very dangerous.
  • 阴亏甚矣 - Means that there is a severe deficiency of yin (cooling and nourishing energy).
  • 必补托而后可 - Means that it must be treated with tonifying and supporting methods.
  • 六味地黄加减 - Refers to a modified version of the Six Flavor Rehmannia Pill, a traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 百钱求邻翁取药 - Means that the wife asked the neighbor to buy medicine for 100 coins.
  • 我家潮湿，甚思买苍术、白芷薰屋 - Indicates that the house is damp, and the neighbor wants to buy *Cangzhu* (苍术) and *Baizhi* (白芷) to dry it out.
  • 兴旺药肆 - Refers to a well-established pharmacy.
  • 投以钱与方并买术、芷二十钱 - Means that the neighbor gave the money and the prescription to the pharmacy and bought *Cangzhu* and *Baizhi* for 20 coins.

  • 若以为有房事者必损，则世无八九十岁夫妇偕老之人，而鳏寡孤独皆千万岁矣 - If sexual intercourse must cause damage, then there would be no long-lived couples.
  • 惟好色过度则伤，黄君一夫一妇，人伦之常，复有何损 - Only excessive indulgence in sex causes harm; Mr. Huang's normal human relationship with one wife cannot cause harm.
  • 其痘之郁而不发者，必平日尽力农田，长浸水中，深受湿气，遏不得达 - The smallpox is suppressed due to working hard in the fields and being deeply affected by dampness.
  • 老翁所市术、芷必有数两，大剂投之，水气立散，有不畅茂条达者乎 - Giving a large dose of artemisia and sweet flag will immediately disperse the dampness and allow the body to flourish.
  • 时医何能见及此 - Modern physicians cannot see this far.
  • 我故曰：祖功宗德，鬼神所使也 - Ancestral merits and virtues are influenced by ghosts and spirits.
  • 且黄君脉大而长，不但痘不复发，且享寿无疑 - Mr. Huang's pulse suggests he will not only be free from smallpox but will also live a long life.
  • 二人心始释然，喜跃再拜而去 - The two men were relieved and left happily.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蛇之种类伙矣 - Classical expression meaning 'the kinds of snakes are numerous.' '伙' is a colloquial term for 'numerous' or 'many.'
  • 追风药也 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'they are all medicines for wind (disease).' In traditional Chinese medicine, 'wind' is a category of illness, and '追风药' (chase-wind medicine) refers to remedies for such conditions.
  • 乌稍蛇 - A specific type of snake, likely referring to the *Agkistrodon acutus* (Chinese pit viper), known for its toxicity. The term '乌稍' may be a variant or regional name for this species.
  • 及笄 - A traditional term for the coming-of-age ceremony for women, usually at age 15, when they are allowed to marry.
  • 过癞去 - A compound verb meaning 'to undergo the process of being afflicted with leprosy (癞) in order to be eligible for marriage.' This is a culturally specific practice.
  • 张灯彩，设筵席，会亲友 - A set phrase describing the preparation of a celebratory event with decorations, food, and the gathering of relatives and friends.
  • 明女癞去可结亲矣 - A formal statement meaning 'it is now clear that the girl has undergone the leprosy process and is eligible to be married.'
  • 浪子亦与宴事毕 - A phrase indicating that the 'wayward youth' (浪子) is also included in the banquet and the formal proceedings.
  • 酌赠医资送去 - A phrase meaning 'to give a gift of money or resources for medical care.'
  • 多则一年，必发癞死 - A conditional statement: 'If [the girl] is with the man for more than a year, she will certainly die of leprosy.'
  • 且能过人，故亲人不敢近 - A compound sentence meaning 'and she can transmit the disease to others, so her relatives dare not get close to her.'
  • 官之好善者，设癞院收养之 - A bureaucratic phrase referring to officials who are 'good-hearted' and establish leprosy hospitals to care for these women.
  • 已冠 - A term referring to a man who has undergone the coming-of-age ceremony (冠礼), usually at age 20.
  • 不好学，日事游荡 - A colloquial expression meaning 'does not like to study and spends his days wandering around.'
  • 戚友知此间风俗者，恒诏戒之 - A formal expression meaning 'relatives and friends who are aware of the local customs often warn him.'
  • 介弟初亦不敢犯 - A phrase meaning 'the younger brother initially dares not to transgress.'
  • 游观而已 - A formal expression meaning 'only to observe and wander.'
  • 国色也 - A classical phrase meaning 'the beauty of the nation,' used to describe an exceptionally beautiful woman.
  • 不粉饰而自然 - A descriptive phrase meaning 'unadorned and naturally beautiful.'
  • 既艳丽而庄重 - A compound description meaning 'both beautiful and dignified.'
  • 不禁迷恋辗转，再三舍之不得 - A phrase indicating intense emotional entanglement: 'unable to resist, he is constantly turning back, unable to let her go.'
  • 喟然曰 - A classical expression meaning 'he sighed and said.'
  • 人生几何，美色难遇 - A poetic expression meaning 'life is short, and beautiful women are hard to find.'
  • 牡丹花下死，较老髦乐甚也 - A poetic and metaphorical saying: 'To die under the peony flower is more enjoyable than to grow old in solitude.' This is a traditional Chinese idiom, often used to express the romantic ideal of dying for love.
  • 与女交谈，引之入室，两情相得，有终焉之志 - A formal narrative structure describing the man's actions and intentions: 'he conversed with the woman, brought her into the room, and the two became mutually fond, with the intention of a lifelong union.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乌蛇已癞 - A title or heading, possibly indicating the subject of the text: 'The Black Snake Has Leprosy.' This may be a metaphorical or symbolic title.
  • 蛇之种类伙矣 - Reflects traditional Chinese natural philosophy, which often categorizes animals and their medicinal properties.
  • 追风药也 - A reference to traditional Chinese medicine, where certain animals (like snakes) are believed to have medicinal properties for treating 'wind' conditions, which include a range of ailments.
  • 乌稍蛇 - A specific type of venomous snake, likely from the genus *Agkistrodon*, known for its potency and danger.
  • 及笄 - A traditional coming-of-age ritual for women, symbolizing readiness for marriage.
  • 过癞去 - A culturally specific practice in the region described (likely southern China), where young women must be exposed to leprosy (possibly metaphorically or through a ritual) in order to be eligible for marriage. This may be a metaphor for a form of social or physical purification or a symbolic rite of passage.
  • 张灯彩，设筵席，会亲友 - Describes a formal wedding-like ceremony, though it is not a marriage itself, but a rite of passage to indicate that the woman is now 'cleansed' and eligible for marriage.
  • 浪子 - Refers to a 'wayward youth' or a man who is not bound by social or familial obligations, often associated with romantic or illicit relationships.
  • 酌赠医资送去 - Indicates a societal expectation or custom where the man provides financial support for the woman’s medical care, possibly as a form of compensation or protection.
  • 癞院 - A term for a leprosy hospital or care facility, indicating that such a condition was recognized and institutionalized in this society.
  • 已冠 - Refers to a man who has undergone the *guanli* (冠礼), the traditional coming-of-age ceremony for men, marking their transition into adulthood.
  • 戚友 - Refers to relatives and close friends.

  • 牡丹花下死，较老髦乐甚也 - A poetic and romantic expression, often used in Chinese literature to express the idea that dying for love is preferable to living a lonely old age.
  • 两情相得，有终焉之志 - A formal expression indicating mutual affection and the desire for a lifelong relationship.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Salt merchants were a powerful economic class in China, particularly during the Ming and Qing dynasties.

  • Heatstroke was a common cause of death in hot climates, especially in historical contexts where medical knowledge was limited.
  • This refers to a practice where individuals could purchase official positions, a common but controversial practice in Chinese history.
  • This metaphor comes from the story of Duke Ling of Jin, who, when captured by the state of Chu, looked dejected and dispirited, leading to the phrase '楚囚对泣' (the prisoners of Chu weeping together).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日 - A time marker meaning 'one day' or 'at one time.'

  • 宅若有妖，何能安居？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'If there is an evil spirit in the house, how can one live in peace?'
  • 此必靈藥所變 - Means 'This must be a transformation by a magical medicine.'
  • 得而蒸食之，当成地仙 - Means 'If you can steam and eat it, you will become an immortal.'
  • 渠闻人声即遁 - Means 'It hears human voices and immediately flees.'

  • 无难 - Means 'It is not difficult.'

  • 妇度院中孩戏之处 - Means 'The woman estimated where the children were playing in the courtyard.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 灵药 - Refers to a magical or supernatural medicine.
  • 地仙 - Refers to an earthly immortal, a being who has achieved a high level of spiritual and physical perfection.
  • 稻米 - Refers to rice, a staple food in Chinese culture, often associated with prosperity and sustenance.
  • 天地正气 - Refers to the harmonious energy of heaven and earth, a concept in traditional Chinese philosophy.
  • 铁锅 - Refers to an iron pot, a common cooking utensil in Chinese households.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence introduces the setting and the main characters: 'One day, a close acquaintance of hers, a learned scholar, came to visit.'
  • This phrase is a polite way of asking for advice: 'She asked him about her doubts.'
  • This rhetorical question and statement introduce the problem and the solution: 'Her mother-in-law said, "If there is an evil spirit in the house, how can one live in peace? This must be a transformation by a magical medicine. If you can steam and eat it, you will become an immortal."'
  • This is a promise of a reward for following the advice: 'If you can steam and eat it, you will become an immortal.'
  • This is a logical argument explaining why the problem is difficult to solve: 'The woman laughed and said, "It hears human voices and immediately flees. How can it be caught?"'
  • This is a counter-argument providing a solution: 'The scholar replied, "It is not difficult. I have heard that rice, which is the essence of the earth and sky, can suppress hidden treasures. If you throw it through the window, and it hits its body, it will not be able to escape."'
  • This is a specific method to solve the problem: 'If you throw it through the window, and it hits its body, it will not be able to escape.'
  • This indicates a change in tone and a shift to the woman's perspective: 'The woman laughed and said, "It hears human voices and immediately flees. How can it be caught?"'
  • This describes the woman's estimation and her initial doubt: 'The woman thought that the children were playing in the courtyard and estimated that the point was about a zhang (about 3 meters) from the window. She thought that throwing the rice might not hit the right spot.'
  • This describes the woman's innovative solution: 'So, she cut a bamboo tube, put the rice inside, and used chopsticks to roll and send it.'
  • This sequence of actions shows the woman's successful execution of her plan: 'She practiced her technique until it was very skillful and tried again at the window. The two children came forward. The woman immediately shot the rice from the tube at them. It hit, and the two children fell down. They were suddenly caught and became stiff and rigid in her hands.'
  • This describes the transformation of the children after being hit by the rice: 'She called her mother-in-law to light a candle. They looked like wooden carvings, with eyebrows and eyes as beautiful as paintings, and had a very fragrant smell.'
  • This describes the process of preparing the children for consumption: 'The mother-in-law and daughter-in-law discussed and decided to steam them in an iron pot while cooking rice. After one steaming, they became slightly soft. After five or six steamings, they became fragrant and soft enough to eat.'
  • This describes the outcome of eating the children: 'The mother-in-law and daughter-in-law each ate one. They found them extremely delicious and felt very full and did not want to eat or drink for the rest of the day.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夜眠至次日，皆不能起身矣 - Literally 'from sleeping at night until the next day, all could not get up,' indicating a prolonged state of unconsciousness or illness.
  • 卓午门不开 - '卓午' refers to noon, and '门不开' means the door was not opened. Together, it suggests a state of inactivity or neglect.
  • 邻姥疑有故 - '邻姥' refers to a neighborly old woman, and '疑有故' means she suspected something was wrong.
  • 逾垣窥之 - '逾垣' means to climb over a wall, and '窥之' means to peek or look. This phrase describes the neighbor's action of investigating.
  • 面及身俱肿 - '面及身俱肿' means both the face and body were swollen.
  • 目开口张，不能言语 - '目开口张' means eyes wide open, and '不能言语' means unable to speak. Together, it describes a state of unconsciousness or severe illness.
  • 邻姥倩人走报 - '倩人' means to ask someone, and '走报' means to run and report. This phrase indicates the neighbor asking someone to inform others.
  • 不解何由，亦不识何疾 - '不解何由' means not understanding the cause, and '亦不识何疾' means also not knowing what disease it was. This phrase describes the confusion and lack of knowledge about the condition.
  • 急邀知医之戚诊视 - '急邀' means to urgently invite, '知医之戚' means a relative who knows medicine, and '诊视' means to diagnose and examine. This phrase indicates the urgency and the involvement of a knowledgeable relative.
  • 未识九蒸九晒之制，又不知避忌，误犯铁器，是以有毒 - '未识九蒸九晒之制' means not knowing the proper preparation method, '又不知避忌' means also not knowing the taboos, '误犯铁器' means mistakenly using iron tools, and '是以有毒' means that is why it was toxic. This phrase explains the cause of the toxicity.
  • 试以解毒开通之药灌之 - '试以' means to try with, '解毒开通之药' means detoxifying and promoting circulation medicine, and '灌之' means to administer. This phrase describes the treatment given.
  • 至七日，肿消人醒 - '至七日' means until the seventh day, '肿消' means the swelling subsided, and '人醒' means the person woke up. This phrase indicates the recovery process.
  • 问之果如医言 - '问之' means to ask, '果如医言' means indeed as the doctor said. This phrase confirms the accuracy of the doctor's diagnosis.
  • 起后，强健逾前，累月不思食 - '起后' means after waking up, '强健逾前' means stronger than before, and '累月不思食' means for months without wanting to eat. This phrase describes the post-recovery state.
  • 其姑年已周甲，发白再黑，齿落重生，枯绉肌肤皆皮脱，而润泽似二十许人，复生子 - '周甲' means reaching the age of 60, '发白再黑' means white hair turning black again, '齿落重生' means lost teeth regrowing, '枯绉肌肤皆皮脱' means dry, wrinkled skin shedding, and '润泽似二十许人' means becoming smooth and moist like a person in their twenties.   • 倘被接掠一空，明日以何物支应 - If we are completely looted, what will we use tomorrow to respond? Rhetorical question expressing the consequences of inaction.
  • 获咎不更重耶 - Won’t the blame be even heavier? Rhetorical question emphasizing the severity of the consequences.
  • 职愿舍一官对之 - I am willing to sacrifice my position to face them. *职* refers to the speaker’s official position or duty.
  • 刺史摇手曰：“我无能为矣，足下善处之。” - The governor shook his head and said, 'I am powerless. You handle it well.' Classical expression of resignation or delegation of authority.
  • 吏目乃唤齐人役，谕曰 - The official then gathered the people and officials and instructed them. *人役* refers to the people or workers under his command.
  • 此去遇监，如我喝打，汝等即擒而行刑 - If you meet the supervisor, if I shout, you must seize and execute. Direct and forceful command.
  • 有我在，罪不及汝，毋畏葸不前，事定后有赏 - As long as I am here, the blame will not fall on you. Do not be timid and hesitate. There will be rewards after the matter is settled. Motivational statement to encourage action.
  • 吏自突前喝问 - The official suddenly stepped forward and shouted a question. *突前* is a strong, sudden movement.
  • 大老爷在此，汝小官何问为？ - The big master is here, why are you, a low official, questioning? Sarcastic or dismissive phrase used by the leader.
  • 喝皂役拘执行刑 - He ordered the constables to arrest and execute. *皂役* refers to the official constables or guards.
  • 监少役多，不能抵拒 - The guards were few, the officials were many, and thus they could not resist. *监* refers to the officials or guards, and *役* refers to the people or workers.
  • 竟将首领摔地，去裤杖责二十，逐出局外，号哭而去 - They finally threw the leader to the ground, stripped him, beat him twenty times with a rod, and expelled him from the hall, where he cried and left. Vivid and graphic description of punishment.
  • 访明官职姓名，在御前泣诉 - They investigated and found out his official title and name, then wept and reported before the emperor. *御前* refers to the presence of the emperor or imperial court.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吏目 - A low-ranking official, often a clerk or assistant in a local government office. Used in the context of the imperial bureaucracy of the Qing or earlier dynasties.
  • 刺史 - A governor or regional administrator in the imperial Chinese system, responsible for overseeing a district or province. Used in the Han, Tang, and later dynasties.
  • 皂役 - Official guards or constables, often dressed in dark uniforms (*皂* meaning black). Part of the local or imperial law enforcement.
  • 首领 - The leader or head of a group, possibly a bandit, official, or subordinate. In the context of the passage, this likely refers to a subordinate or rival official.
  • 帐房 - A term for an office or room where accounts or financial matters are handled. *帐* refers to accounts or ledgers, and *房* is a room or office.
  • 银物 - Silver and goods, referring to valuable items or money.
  • 御前 - The presence of the emperor or the imperial court. High-level term indicating a formal audience with the emperor.
  • 杖责二十 - A punishment involving twenty lashes with a rod, a common form of corporal punishment in imperial China.
  • 号哭而去 - To weep and leave, a dramatic and emotional expression of shame or defeat.
  • 访明官职姓名 - To investigate and clarify the official title and name. Common procedure in imperial courts to verify the identity and status of individuals.

Proper Nouns

  • 吏目 - A title for a low-ranking official. Should be translated as 'clerk,' 'assistant official,' or 'low-ranking official' depending on context.
  • 刺史 - A title for a regional governor. Should be translated as 'governor' or 'regional administrator.'
  • 御前 - A term referring to the emperor's presence. Should be translated as 'before the emperor' or 'in the imperial court.'
  • 杖责二十 - A specific punishment. Should be translated as 'twenty lashes with a rod' or 'beaten twenty times with a rod.'
  • 首领 - A term for a leader or head of a group. Should be translated as 'leader,' 'chief,' or 'head.'
  • 帐房 - A term for an office or room where accounts are handled. Should be translated as 'account office' or 'financial room.'
  • 皂役 - A term for official guards or constables. Should be translated as 'constables' or 'official guards.'

  • 参苓 - Ginseng and poria, two important herbs in traditional Chinese medicine known for their medicinal properties.
  • 糜 - A type of porridge, often used in traditional Chinese medicine as a nourishing food.
  • 真姓名 - Refers to the monk's real name, which is not mentioned in the text, indicating a level of respect or secrecy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence '一日，有舁一垂毙之人至' introduces the main event, which must be clearly understood to follow the narrative.
  • The phrase '腹如孕' describes the patient's condition, which must be interpreted correctly to understand the severity of the illness.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 已成名手 - Has become a master; refers to someone highly skilled in their craft.
  • 彼后生小子 - Derogatory term for young, inexperienced people; literally 'those young men' or 'these young upstarts.'
  • 居然吾道在是 - And yet they think our art is in their hands; implies a false sense of authority or mastery.
  • 大胆行医 - Dare to practice medicine; suggests recklessness or lack of proper qualification.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 叶天士 - Renowned physician of the Qing dynasty, known for his expertise in traditional Chinese medicine and contributions to *Yi Lin Gai Cuo* (Medical Errors Corrected).
  • 《脉诀》 - Classical medical text on pulse diagnosis; widely used by physicians to diagnose diseases based on pulse patterns.
  • 《本草》 - General term for herbal medical texts, often referring to *Bencao Gangmu* or *Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing*.
  • 后生小子 - Term used in classical Chinese to refer to young men, often with a condescending or critical tone, suggesting they are inexperienced or overconfident.
  • 吾道 - 'Our art' or 'our way,' referring to the medical profession and its traditions, implying a sense of responsibility and continuity.

Proper Nouns

  • 芗厈 - Uncertain; may be a personal name or title. If a name, should be transliterated as *Xiang Yan* or *Xiang Tan*, depending on the character forms used.
  • 叶天士 - Well-known historical figure; should be rendered as *Ye Tianshi*.
  • 《脉诀》 - Classical medical text on pulse diagnosis; should be transliterated as *Mai Jue*.
  • 《本草》 - General term for herbal medical texts; should be transliterated as *Ben Cao*.

The earth god warns that the troupe leader is among those who will die from the plague and cannot be pursued.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某少君 - A conventional way of referring to a young gentleman or noble youth. '某' is a placeholder for a name, and '少君' is a respectful term for a young man of noble or scholarly background.
  • 能詩文而未遇 - Able to compose poetry and prose but has not yet found fortune or opportunity. This reflects a common motif in classical literature of talented individuals who have not yet achieved recognition or success.
  • 翩翩美少年也 - A poetic and complimentary description of a 'graceful and handsome young man.'
  • 行至羊腸阪 - Traveled to the winding pass (or narrow mountain path). '羊腸阪' is a classical term for a narrow, winding mountain path, often associated with danger or difficulty.
  • 馬逸 - The horse ran wild or the horse bolted. '逸' here means to run off or escape.
  • 身糜而魂出 - His body was shattered, and his soul departed. '糜' means to be shattered or disintegrated.
  • 隨風飄盪，瞬息數千里 - Followed the wind, drifting for thousands of miles in an instant. This is a poetic and metaphorical way of describing the soul's journey after death.
  • 求止不得 - Unable to find a place to rest or unable to find a resting place.
  • 忽墮於山东历城县村 - Suddenly fell into a village in Licheng County, Shandong. This is a specific geographic reference, which should be translated with care.
  • 落間初死男尸壳中 - Fell into the body of a recently deceased male corpse. '尸壳' can be interpreted as the empty shell or husk of a corpse, suggesting the soul has taken over the body of a dead man.
  • 氣絕逾日，如何得醒？ - His breath had ceased for over a day; how could he possibly revive? This is a rhetorical question expressing disbelief at the revival.
  • 氣暖身和，復生无疑 - His breath is warm, his body is harmonious; his revival is certain. This is a diagnostic statement used in traditional Chinese medicine to determine if someone is alive or dead.
  • 頻聞欢乐聲 - Frequently heard the sound of joy. Indicates the family's celebration.
  • 我兒何云跌死，曷為我言之 - My son, why do you say you fell to your death? Why do you speak to me? This is a question from the mother, expressing confusion and concern.
  • 渠雖少蘇，神尚未完 - Though he has slightly revived, his spirit is not yet whole. '渠' is a classical pronoun meaning 'he.' '少蘇' means 'slightly revived.'
  • 此汝母，如何不識 - This is your mother; how could you not recognize her? A rhetorical question indicating the confusion of the revived person.
  • 谬甚！谬甚！ - How absurd! How absurd! An exclamation of disbelief or confusion.
  • 随父莅任 - Accompanied his father on his official duties. '莅任' means to take up an official post or duty.
  • 行蜀道上，堕马被风吹至此 - While traveling on the Sichuan road, I fell from my horse and was blown by the wind to this place. This is the revived man's explanation of how he came to be in this body.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 少君 - A respectful term for a young man, often used in classical literature to denote a noble or scholarly youth.
  • 科甲铨 - Refers to the imperial examination system. '科甲' refers to the examination ranks (e.g., jinshi, juren), and '铨' refers to the selection or appointment of officials. This indicates that the father was selected as a county magistrate through the examination system.
  • 四川县令 - A county magistrate in Sichuan province. This is a specific administrative title from the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 羊腸阪 - A classical term for a narrow, winding mountain pass, often used in literature to symbolize difficulty or danger.
  • 山东历城县 - A specific county in Shandong province, indicating a real geographic location. This should be translated with the modern name, 'Licheng County, Shandong Province.'
  • 气绝逾日 - Refers to the traditional Chinese medical concept of determining death based on the absence of breath and body warmth. In this context, the person was presumed dead for more than a day.
  • 气暖身和 - A diagnostic sign in traditional Chinese medicine indicating that a person is alive. This is used to confirm the revival of the young man.
  • 父老 - Refers to elderly men or local elders, often used in classical texts to denote respected community figures.
  • 丑妇 - A woman described as unattractive. In classical Chinese, this term can carry connotations of social status or marital situation.
  • 村童 - A village child, likely the son of the revived man.
  • 随父莅任 - Indicates that the young man was accompanying his father on his official duties, a common practice in imperial China.
  • 行蜀道上 - Refers to traveling on the Sichuan road, which was known for its dangerous mountain passes and was a common route in classical Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 少君 - A respectful term for a young man, not a proper name. It should be translated as 'a young gentleman' or 'a noble youth.'
  • 某 - A placeholder for a name, used in classical Chinese to avoid specifying a name directly.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This clause describes the subject's recent changes.

  • This clause expresses the old man's concern about the subject's reputation.
  • This clause predicts the consequences of the subject's actions.
  • This clause raises the practical concerns of the subject's situation.
  • This phrase indicates the old man's intention to offer advice.
  • This clause is a response to the old man's advice, expressing a desire to understand the true meaning.
  • This clause explains the old man's understanding of the subject's situation.
  • This clause uses a metaphor to advise the subject to act according to their new identity.
  • This clause provides an analogy to illustrate the importance of adhering to one's role.
  • This clause concludes the advice by emphasizing the social consequences of not following the old man's counsel.

  • 赴试 - Refers to taking an examination, often in the context of the imperial examination system.
  • 旋入学为名诸生 - Means 'soon enrolled in school as a prominent student.'
  • 禀告乃翁 - Means 'to inform one's father.'
  • 大令奇其事 - Means 'the magistrate found the matter strange.'
  • 寄资作札招之去 - Means 'sent money and a letter inviting him to leave.'
  • 堕马死 - Means 'died from falling off a horse.'
  • 衰恸 - Means 'to grieve deeply.'
  • 窃喜 - Means 'to secretly rejoice.'
  • 貌不扬 - Means 'unattractive in appearance.'
  • 狐疑 - Means 'to be in doubt.'
  • 妄冒 - Means 'to falsely claim' or 'to pretend.'
  • 历举幼时游戏事及父母秘密之言 - Means 'to list in detail childhood games and secret words of his parents.'
  • 委曲道达 - Means 'to explain in detail.'
  • 垂怜 - Means 'to show pity or sympathy.'
  • 决意逐之 - Means 'to decide to drive him away.'
  • 私与千金遣归山左 - Means 'privately gave him a thousand gold pieces and sent him back to the eastern mountains.'
  • 家以小康 - Means 'his family became moderately wealthy.'
  • 谈者曰此人现在，后不知作何结局矣 - Means 'people say he is still alive now, but it is unknown what his future holds.'
  • 异哉 - Exclamation meaning 'strange indeed.'
  • 翩翩佳公子 - Means 'a handsome young nobleman.'
  • 窭人 - Means 'a poor person.'
  • 膴仕 - Means 'a high-ranking official.'
  • 投荒 - Means 'to be exiled to a remote place.'
  • 造化弄人 - Means 'fate plays tricks on people.'
  • 极矣 - Means 'to the extreme.'
  • 不弟之显罚也 - Means 'a clear punishment for his disrespect.'
  • 乃兄 - Means 'his elder brother.'
  • 受侮不少 - Means 'suffered much humiliation.'
  • 现身作法 - Means 'to serve as an example.'
  • 示鉴于人伦 - Means 'to show a lesson in human relationships.'
  • 一失足成千古恨 - Means 'one misstep can lead to eternal regret.'
  • 再回头已百年身 - Means 'turning back would be like starting over after a hundred years.'
  • 为少君诵矣 - Means 'let this be a lesson for the young man.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赴试 - Refers to the imperial examination system, which was a crucial part of Chinese society for selecting government officials.
  • 名诸生 - Refers to students who were recognized for their academic excellence.
  • 大令 - Refers to a magistrate, a local government official.
  • 山左 - Refers to the eastern part of China, particularly Shandong province.
  • 造化弄人 - A common phrase in Chinese literature expressing the idea that fate or destiny can be capricious and unfair.
  • 一失足成千古恨 - A proverb warning against the consequences of a single mistake.
  • 再回头已百年身 - Another proverb emphasizing the irreversibility of certain actions and their consequences.

Proper Nouns

  • 少君 - The name of the young man.
  • 大令 - The name of the magistrate.
  • 乃翁 - Refers to the young man's father.
  • 乃兄 - Refers to the young man's elder brother.
  • 母 - Refers to the young man's mother.
  • 山左 - A geographical term referring to the eastern part of China, particularly Shandong province.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾鄉 - My hometown or the people of my hometown, used to introduce the subject of the narrative.
  • 許大宗伯 - Xu, the Grand Censor; a high-ranking official in the ancient Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 幼失怙恃 - Lost his parents at a young age; a common phrase in Classical Chinese to describe orphanhood.
  • 終鮮兄弟 - No brothers or sisters; indicating the subject's lack of siblings.
  • 室如懸罄 - The house is as empty as a suspended bell; a metaphor for extreme poverty.
  • 地無立锥 - No place to stand a cone; another metaphor for extreme poverty or lack of land.
  • 依寺僧訓蒙度日 - Depended on a temple monk to teach and support himself; describing the subject's early life.
  • 每聞僧作法事歸 - Every time the monk returned from performing rituals; referring to religious ceremonies.
  • 聚論人家字畫 - Gathered to discuss people's paintings and calligraphy; indicating a common topic of conversation among the monks.
  • 堂幅 - Large hanging scroll; a type of traditional Chinese painting or calligraphy.
  • 單條 - Vertical scroll; another type of traditional Chinese painting or calligraphy.
  • 橫披 - Horizontal scroll; yet another type of traditional Chinese painting or calligraphy.
  • 楹聯 - Couplet; a pair of parallel sentences often used as decorative writing.
  • 隱語 - Cryptic language or coded speech; used here to describe the monks' indirect way of discussing people.
  • 正妻 - Principal wife; the primary wife in a household.
  • 妾 - Concubine; a secondary wife in a household.
  • 女 - Daughter; referring to the subject's daughter.
  • 婢 - Maid; a female servant.
  • 嗣應試 - Later took the exams; referring to the subject's participation in the imperial examinations.
  • 屢冠童子軍 - Often ranked first in the preliminary exams; indicating the subject's academic success.
  • 苦不得售 - Struggled to pass; describing the subject's difficulty in passing the exams.
  • 年將而立 - Near the age of thirty; referring to the subject's age.
  • 無與論婚者 - No one to discuss marriage with; indicating the subject's lack of marital prospects.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 許大宗伯 - Xu Rulin, a historical figure who served as a Grand Censor in the Ming dynasty; the title '大宗伯' is a high-ranking official in the ancient Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 寺僧 - Monks who lived in temples, often involved in religious and educational activities.
  • 法事 - Religious rituals or ceremonies performed by monks.
  • 字畫 - Traditional Chinese paintings and calligraphy, which were highly valued in Chinese culture.
  • 隱語 - A form of coded or cryptic language, often used to convey indirect or hidden meanings.
  • 童子軍 - Preliminary exams for the imperial examinations, which were the primary means of selecting government officials in China.

Proper Nouns

  • 許大宗伯 - Xu Rulin, a historical figure who served as a Grand Censor in the Ming dynasty.
  • 童子軍 - Preliminary exams for the imperial examinations.

[558]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鄉有葭莩亲 - In the village, distant relatives or acquaintances, often used to describe people who are not closely related but are still considered part of the community.
  • 以耕读为业 - A lifestyle that combines agricultural work with scholarly pursuits, reflecting the traditional Chinese value of a balanced life.
  • 时全家患疫 - The entire family suffered from an epidemic, indicating a historical context where disease outbreaks were common and had significant social impacts.
  • 惟一女无恙 - The survival of the daughter despite the illness of the rest of the family may be seen as a sign of divine favor or good fortune.
  • 戚翁静卧床中 - The elderly relative lying quietly in bed, reflecting the importance of respect and care for elders in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 夜闻众鬼议曰 - The ghosts discussing their hiding place at night, adding a mystical dimension to the story, reflecting beliefs in the spiritual world.
  • 明日许宗伯来视疾 - The visit of a respected official named Xu Zongbo to check on the illness, underscoring the social status and connections of the family.
  • 我曹避何处 - The ghosts asking where they can hide, reflecting their fear and uncertainty.
  • 翁床下大瓮 - The large jar under the old man's bed used as a hiding place for the ghosts, reflecting the resourcefulness and ingenuity of the characters.
  • 聚处其中，亦可暂避一时耳 - The ghosts gathering in the jar, which can temporarily hide them for a while.
  • 是日，公闻戚病，果来探视 - That day, the official heard about the relative's illness and indeed came to visit.
  • 翁见公大悦 - The old man was very pleased to see the official.
  • 使其女奉以馔，甚丰 - He had his daughter serve food, which was very abundant.
  • 公不安欲去 - The official felt uneasy and wanted to leave.
  • 翁跃起曰 - The old man jumped up and said.
  • 有事烦君，看老夫薄面万不可辞 - There is something I need your help with, for the sake of my old face, you must not refuse.
  • 请以纸硃书‘大宗伯封’四字，封老夫床下瓮口，举而投诸河 - Please write the four characters '大宗伯封' on paper with vermilion ink, seal the mouth of the large jar under my bed, and throw it into the river.
  • 归，病者果痊愈 - The official returned, and the sick person indeed recovered.
  • 翁益喜，遣媒以女订婚 - The old man became even happier and sent a matchmaker to arrange a marriage for his daughter.
  • 年齿皆长，急须聘娶 - They were both of marriageable age and urgently needed to get married.
  • 翁为公作大布衣冠，草草成礼 - The old man made simple clothes and a hat for the official, and the wedding ceremony was hastily conducted.
  • 公无力备花烛 - The official could not afford the wedding candles.
  • 代以二油灯 - Instead, he used two oil lamps.
  • 其夫人恒曰 - His wife often said.
  • 妾每见取妇家，必彩舆、鼓乐、鸣炮 - I always see the bride's family with colorful sedan chairs, music, and firecrackers.
  • 君今一事不备，岂非妾命累君耶 - You have nothing prepared today, isn't this because of my bad luck?
  • 公曰 - The official said.
  • 卿今羡此不得，恐异日闻之生厌耳 - You can't envy this now, but I fear you will grow tired of hearing about it in the future.
  • 夫人曰 - His wife said.
  • 妾乐此不疲，何厌之有 - I enjoy it without tiring, why should I be tired of it?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乡有葭莩亲 - Reflects the social structure of traditional Chinese villages, where relationships extended beyond close family ties to include distant relatives and acquaintances.
  • 以耕读为业 - Highlights the traditional Chinese value of combining agricultural work with scholarly pursuits, reflecting the Confucian ideal of a balanced life.
  • 时全家患疫 - Suggests a historical context where disease outbreaks were common and had significant social impacts.
  • 惟一女无恙 - The survival of the daughter despite the illness of the rest of the family may be seen as a sign of divine favor or good fortune.
  • 戚翁静卧床中 - The elderly relative's condition and the family's reaction reflect the importance of respect and care for elders in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 夜闻众鬼议曰 - The supernatural element of the ghosts discussing their hiding place adds a mystical dimension to the story, reflecting beliefs in the spiritual world.
  • 明日许宗伯来视疾 - The visit of a respected official (许宗伯) underscores the social status and connections of the family.
  • 翁床下大瓮 - The use of a large jar as a hiding place for the ghosts is a creative and culturally resonant detail, reflecting the resourcefulness and ingenuity of the characters.
  • 归，病者果痊愈 - The recovery of the sick person after the official's intervention suggests a belief in the power of good deeds and the supernatural.
  • 翁益喜，遣媒以女订婚 - The arrangement of a marriage for the daughter reflects the importance of family alliances and social obligations in traditional Chinese society.
  • 年齿皆长，急须聘娶 - The urgency of the marriage reflects the social pressure to marry at an appropriate age.
  • 翁为公作大布衣冠，草草成礼 - The simplicity of the wedding ceremony reflects the modesty and practicality of the characters.
  • 公无力备花烛 - The inability to afford traditional wedding candles highlights the economic constraints of the characters.
  • 代以二油灯 - The use of oil lamps instead of candles is a practical solution that also reflects the characters' resourcefulness.
  • 其夫人恒曰 - The wife's comments reflect the cultural expectations and pressures placed on women in traditional Chinese society.
  • 妾每见取妇家，必彩舆、鼓乐、鸣炮 - The description of a typical wedding highlights the cultural norms and traditions surrounding marriage.
  • 君今一事不备，岂非妾命累君耶 - The wife's concern about her bad luck reflects the cultural belief in the influence of fate and the importance of a harmonious marriage.
  • 卿今羡此不得，恐异日闻之生厌耳 - The husband's response reflects a pragmatic and philosophical approach to life, emphasizing the importance of contentment and adaptability.
  • 妾乐此不疲，何厌之有 - The wife's response reflects her contentment and satisfaction with their simple life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未幾 - Classical adverb meaning 'not long after' or 'soon after.'
  • 入泮 - Term referring to a scholar's enrollment in the local school (the 泮宮), a rite of passage for those who have passed the county-level examination.
  • 應秋闱 - To participate in the autumn examination (the 秋闱), one of the regular imperial examinations.
  • 無力買舟 - Literally 'without the means to buy a boat,' meaning lacking the funds to hire a boat for travel.
  • 趁夜航之便 - To take advantage of the convenience of night ferry services.
  • 行百里不過數十錢 - Hyperbolic expression meaning that traveling a hundred miles cost only a few coins, emphasizing the cheapness of the night ferry.
  • 無卧具者，倒不准趁船 - A rule stating that those without sleeping gear (卧具) are not allowed to board the boat.
  • 盍以敝毡裹兩破蒲團，用充卧具 - A suggestion to use a worn felt mat wrapped around two broken 蒲團 (a type of mat or cushion) as a substitute for sleeping gear.
  • 公如法趁船赴省 - The person followed this method and boarded the boat to go to the provincial capital.
  • 停泊時 - When the boat was docked or moored.
  • 舟子舉其具擲岸上樹間 - The boatman took the sleeping gear and threw it among the trees on the shore.
  • 公亦不顧 - The person did not look back or care.
  • 入茶肆 - Went into a tea house or tea shop.
  • 旋有驿卒乘骑至 - Soon after, a postal official (驿卒) arrived on horseback.
  • 系马树下 - Tied the horse under a tree.
  • 马饥，见破毡内有草，蹄而食之 - The horse was hungry and saw grass inside the broken felt mat, then stamped on it and ate it.
  • 公亦大笑而去 - The person laughed and left.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 入泮 - Refers to the traditional Confucian educational system, where students who passed the county-level examination were admitted to the local school (泮宮), symbolizing the beginning of a scholar’s journey.
  • 秋闱 - One of the regular imperial examinations held in the autumn. These were part of the gaokao (high examination) system used to select government officials.
  • 夜航 - Refers to night ferries or night boats, a common and inexpensive means of transport for poor scholars traveling to examination centers.
  • 卧具 - Sleeping gear or bedding, a requirement for boarding a boat in this context.
  • 蒲团 - A type of mat or cushion made of reeds, commonly used in ancient China for sitting or sleeping.
  • 驿卒 - A postal official or courier in the imperial postal system, responsible for delivering messages and transporting goods.
  • 驿 - The imperial postal station system, which was crucial for communication and transportation in ancient China.
  • 许爷 - A respectful term for 'Master Xu,' indicating the person's name or title. The name 'Xu' is likely a surname, though it is not explicitly stated.
  • 茶肆 - A tea house or tea shop, common gathering places in ancient Chinese towns.

Proper Nouns

  • 许爷 - Likely refers to a person named 'Xu,' with '爷' being a respectful honorific. This is a proper name or title.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 举孝廉 - A term referring to the Han dynasty's system of selecting officials based on moral character and scholarship.
  • 捷南宫 - A metaphor for passing the imperial examinations and entering the government.
  • 入词林 - Refers to entering the literary or scholarly community.
  • 转春坊 - A reference to a change in official position, likely within the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 由卿贰外转方伯 - Indicates a promotion from a lower-ranking official to a higher position, possibly a regional governor.
  • 旋晋中丞 - A further promotion to a high-ranking official, likely in a capital city.
  • 中丞官廨限于地 - Describes the limited space of the official residence of the Zhongcheng (a high-ranking official).
  • 横而长 - A description of the layout of the residence.
  • 上房距鼓亭炮台密迩 - Indicates that the main building is close to the drum tower and artillery platform.
  • 早晚鼓吹鸣炮 - Refers to the daily rituals of drumming, music, and cannon fire.
  • 夫人不得安眠 - Indicates that the wife is unable to sleep due to the noise.
  • 盍命稍停乎 - A polite suggestion to stop or reduce the noise.
  • 卿昔求之不得，今果厌烦耶 - A rhetorical question implying that the wife once desired the noise but now finds it annoying.
  • 我所以日日为此者，非自鸣得意，所以补卿昔日之不足耳 - Explains that the official is not showing off but compensating for the wife's past dissatisfaction.
  • 卿既足矣，从此收声，不仅稍停而已 - Indicates that the wife's needs are now met, and the noise will be stopped completely.
  • 果内召为大宗伯 - A promotion to a high-ranking official, likely in the imperial court.
  • 妻封一品夫人 - Indicates that the wife is granted the title of First-Rank Lady.
  • 公卒时遗教子孙，永不许延僧作佛事 - The official leaves a will instructing his descendants not to invite monks for Buddhist rituals.
  • 违者以不孝论 - Indicates that violating this will is considered a form of disrespect or filial impiety.
  • 实有憾于字画之说也 - Suggests that the official has reservations about the practice of painting or calligraphy.
  • 芗厈曰 - A reference to the author or commentator, likely a scholar or historian.
  • 许公未达时，可谓君子固穷 - A commentary on the official's character, suggesting that he was virtuous even in poverty.
  • 吾乡人至今称物之不堪者，曰“许老爷铺盖。” - A colloquial expression indicating that the official's possessions were of poor quality.
  • 又曰：“许老爷鼓吹，补补你。” - Another colloquial expression, suggesting that the official's noise was meant to compensate for something.
  • 竟成谚语 - Indicates that these expressions have become common sayings.
  • 至其文集，为国初名家政绩 - Refers to the official's literary works, which are considered important historical records.
  • 足以上动圣明 - Suggests that the official's works are so valuable that they could move even the most enlightened rulers.
  • 不但能言之者少，即知之者亦稀，何风俗之陋耶 - A commentary on the rarity of those who can or understand the official's works, suggesting a decline in cultural values.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉 - A system of selecting officials based on moral character and scholarship in the Han dynasty.
  • 南宫 - The imperial palace or a metaphor for the imperial examinations.
  • 词林 - The literary or scholarly community.
  • 春坊 - A reference to a specific department or position within the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 卿贰 - A term for lower-ranking officials.
  • 方伯 - A term for a regional governor.
  • 中丞 - A high-ranking official, likely in a capital city.
  • 鼓亭炮台 - Refers to the drum tower and artillery platform, which were part of the daily rituals in imperial cities.
  • 大宗伯 - A high-ranking official, likely in the imperial court.
  • 一品夫人 - The title of First-Rank Lady, granted to the wives of high-ranking officials.
  • 字画 - Refers to calligraphy or painting, which were highly valued in Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 许公 - The name of the official being discussed.
  • 芗厈 - The name of the author or commentator.
